# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Armature Reaction in a Synchronous Generator. How does it affect the main field flux at (i) unity power factor and (ii) 0.8 power factor lagging?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Armature reaction is the effect of the rotating MMF produced by the 3-phase armature currents on the main field MMF produced by the DC-excited rotor winding. It modifies the magnitude and waveform of the resultant air-gap flux.

**(i) Unity Power Factor:** The armature MMF is purely **cross-magnetising** — it distorts the main flux wave but does not weaken or strengthen its magnitude (no net change in flux, only waveform distortion).

**(ii) 0.8 Power Factor Lagging:** The armature MMF has **two components** — a cross-magnetising component and a **demagnetising component** along the main field axis. The net effect is **weakening of the main field flux**, leading to a reduction in the induced EMF E₀.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Synchronous Impedance? How is it determined experimentally using OCC and SCC?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Synchronous impedance $Z_s$ is a fictitious impedance per phase of a synchronous machine, comprising the actual armature resistance $R_a$ and the synchronous reactance $X_s = X_{al} + X_{ar}$, such that the voltage drop $I_a Z_s$ accounts for the total electromagnetic effect on the internal EMF.

**Experimental Determination:**

1. Drive the machine at rated synchronous speed Nₛ by a prime mover.
2. With armature terminals **open**, vary the field current $I_f$ and record the open-circuit EMF E₀ → plot OCC.
3. With armature terminals **short-circuited through an ammeter**, vary $I_f$ and record the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ → plot SCC (linear).
4. At any chosen field current $I_f$:
$$Z_{s} = \frac{E_{0(open)}}{I_{sc}} \text{ (per phase)}$$
5. Measure $R_a$ by voltmeter-ammeter test (or DC drop method); then:
$$X_{s} = \sqrt{Z_{s}^{2} - R_{a}^{2}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with the help of a phasor diagram the effect of armature reaction on the main field flux of a synchronous generator at lagging, unity, and leading power factors. Derive the EMF equation of a synchronous generator.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 10-pole, star-connected cylindrical rotor synchronous generator has 180 slots with 6 conductors per slot. The coils are short-pitched by 2 slots. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. If the armature resistance per phase is 0.4 Ω, find the induced EMF per phase at no-load and the synchronous reactance per phase given the synchronous impedance is 6 Ω.

#### Model Solution — (a)

**1. Effect of Armature Reaction (Phasor Approach):**

Let V be the terminal voltage (reference), and $E_0$ the no-load EMF (which is along the direct axis — the field axis). The armature current $I_a$ lags V by φ (load pf angle).

The armature MMF $\mathcal{F}_{ar}$ is aligned with the stator current phasor. We decompose $I_a$ into two components:

$$I_{d} = I_a \sin\psi \quad \text{(along } E_0 \text{ direction)}$$
$$I_{q} = I_a \cos\psi \quad \text{(perpendicular to } E_0\text{)}$$

Where ψ is the angle between $E_0$ and V (= load angle δ for cylindrical rotor). For a simple picture, if we approximate $E_0 \approx V$, then $I_d$ is the demagnetising component (in phase with $E_0$) and $I_q$ is the cross-magnetising component (quadrature to $E_0$).

**Phasor Diagram Summary:**

| Load | Effect on Main Flux |
|---|---|
| Lagging pf | Net demagnetising + cross-magnetising → **flux weakened & distorted** |
| Unity pf | Pure cross-magnetising → **flux distorted only, no net weakening** |
| Leading pf | Net magnetising + cross-magnetising → **flux strengthened & distorted** |

**2. Derivation of EMF Equation:**

Already derived in detail in Section 3.1 above. Concluding form:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, T_{ph} \, k_{w}$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given Data:**
- 3-phase, 50 Hz, P = 10 poles, star-connected
- S = 180 slots, 6 conductors/slot
- Coils short-pitched by 2 slots
- Φ = 0.05 Wb
- $R_a$ = 0.4 Ω per phase
- $Z_s$ = 6 Ω per phase

**Step 1 — Number of conductors per phase:**

$$Z_{\text{total}} = 180 \times 6 = 1080 \text{ conductors}$$

Since the machine is 3-phase, conductors per phase:

$$Z_{ph} = \frac{1080}{3} = 360 \text{ conductors}$$

**Step 2 — Turns per phase** (each turn = 2 conductors):

$$T_{ph} = \frac{Z_{ph}}{2} = \frac{360}{2} = 180 \text{ turns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute winding factor k_w:**

- **Slots per pole** $S_p = S/P = 180/10 = 18$
- **Slots per pole per phase** $m = S/(P \times 3) = 180/(10 \times 3) = 6$
- **Slot angle** $\gamma = 180°/(S/P) = 180°/18 = 10°$ (electrical)
- **Distribution factor** $k_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\gamma/2)}{m\sin(\gamma/2)} = \dfrac{\sin(6 \times 10°/2)}{6\sin(10°/2)} = \dfrac{\sin 30°}{6 \sin 5°} = \dfrac{0.5}{6 \times 0.08716} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5229} = 0.9561$

- **Coil pitch:** Full pitch = slots per pole = 18 slots = 180° electrical. Short-pitched by 2 slots → actual pitch = 16 slots.
- **Chording angle** $\alpha = (2/18) \times 180° = 20°$
- **Pitch factor** $k_p = \cos(\alpha/2) = \cos(10°) = 0.9848$

- **Winding factor** $k_w = k_d \times k_p = 0.9561 \times 0.9848 = 0.9417$

**Step 4 — Compute no-load EMF per phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 180 \times 0.9417$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 180 \times 0.9417$$

$$E_{ph} = 222 \times 0.05 \times 180 \times 0.9417 \approx 1881.7 \, \text{V (RMS per phase)}$$

Wait — let us re-evaluate. $4.44 \times 50 = 222$; $222 \times 0.05 = 11.1$; $11.1 \times 180 = 1998$; $1998 \times 0.9417 \approx 1881.5$ V.

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 1881.5 \, \text{V per phase}}$$

(Line EMF $E_L = \sqrt{3} \times 1881.5 \approx 3259$ V.)

**Step 5 — Compute synchronous reactance:**

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{6^2 - 0.4^2} = \sqrt{36 - 0.16} = \sqrt{35.84}$$

$$\boxed{X_s \approx 5.987 \, \Omega \text{ per phase}}$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat phasor diagram, derive the expression for the no-load EMF $E_0$ of a loaded synchronous generator in terms of terminal voltage V, armature current $I_a$, armature resistance $R_a$ and synchronous reactance $X_s$. Hence explain the EMF method of finding voltage regulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 5 kVA, 220 V, star-connected synchronous generator has armature resistance of 0.3 Ω per phase. A field current of 2 A produces an open-circuit EMF of 220 V per phase and a short-circuit current of 25 A per phase. Find: (i) Synchronous impedance and synchronous reactance per phase, (ii) Voltage regulation at 0.8 power factor lagging, and (iii) Voltage regulation at 0.8 power factor leading.

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Phasor Diagram Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.3 above. The fundamental phasor equation for a loaded cylindrical rotor synchronous generator is:

$$E_0 = V + I_a (R_a + j X_s)$$

**EMF Method of Voltage Regulation:**

1. From OCC and SCC, determine $Z_s = E_0/I_{sc}$ at the desired field current.
2. With $V$ as reference, draw $I_a$ at angle φ (lagging/leading/unity).
3. Construct $I_a R_a$ parallel to $I_a$ and $I_a X_s$ perpendicular to $I_a$.
4. Use phasor addition to find $\vert E_0\vert$.
5. Compute:
$$\%VR = \frac{\vert E_0 \vert - \vert V \vert}{\vert V \vert} \times 100$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given Data:**
- 3-phase, S = 5 kVA, V_L = 220 V, star-connected
- $R_a$ = 0.3 Ω/phase
- At $I_f$ = 2 A: $E_{0(open)}$ = 220 V/phase, $I_{sc}$ = 25 A/phase
- pf = 0.8 lag and 0.8 lead

**Step 1 — Per phase rated current:**

$$I_a = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} V_L} = \frac{5000}{\sqrt{3} \times 220} = \frac{5000}{381.05} = 13.12 \, \text{A per phase}$$

Per phase rated voltage:

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{3}} = 127.0 \, \text{V per phase}$$

**(i) Synchronous Impedance and Reactance:**

$$Z_s = \frac{E_{0(open)}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{220}{25} = 8.8 \, \Omega \text{ per phase}$$

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{8.8^2 - 0.3^2} = \sqrt{77.44 - 0.09} = \sqrt{77.35}$$

$$X_s \approx 8.795 \, \Omega \text{ per phase}$$

**(ii) Voltage Regulation at 0.8 Lagging Power Factor:**

Here $\cos\phi = 0.8$ and $\sin\phi = 0.6$.

$$V\cos\phi = 127.0 \times 0.8 = 101.6 \, \text{V}$$
$$V\sin\phi = 127.0 \times 0.6 = 76.2 \, \text{V}$$
$$I_a R_a = 13.12 \times 0.3 = 3.936 \, \text{V}$$
$$I_a X_s = 13.12 \times 8.795 = 115.39 \, \text{V}$$

Magnitude of $E_0$ (lagging pf formula):

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{(101.6 + 3.936)^2 + (76.2 + 115.39)^2}$$

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{(105.536)^2 + (191.59)^2}$$

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{11137.8 + 36707.7} = \sqrt{47845.5} \approx 218.7 \, \text{V}$$

Voltage regulation:

$$\%VR_{lag} = \frac{218.7 - 127.0}{127.0} \times 100 = \frac{91.7}{127.0} \times 100 \approx 72.2\%$$

**(iii) Voltage Regulation at 0.8 Leading Power Factor:**

For leading pf, the formula has $-V\sin\phi$ (because the reactive drop effectively subtracts):

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (-V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{(101.6 + 3.936)^2 + (-76.2 + 115.39)^2}$$

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{(105.536)^2 + (39.19)^2}$$

$$\vert E_{0} \vert = \sqrt{11137.8 + 1535.9} = \sqrt{12673.7} \approx 112.6 \, \text{V}$$

Voltage regulation:

$$\%VR_{lead} = \frac{112.6 - 127.0}{127.0} \times 100 = \frac{-14.4}{127.0} \times 100 \approx -11.34\%$$

**Interpretation:** The negative regulation at leading pf confirms that for over-excited machines supplying leading-pf loads (or capacitive loads), the terminal voltage actually **rises** when load is removed — a known characteristic of synchronous generators feeding capacitive loads (e.g., long transmission lines, underground cables).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of sin φ term:** Many students write $+V\sin\phi$ for leading pf. The correct expression has $-\vert V\sin\phi \vert$ because the armature current now leads V, shifting the reactive drop vector in the *opposite* direction. Losing 2 marks here is very common.
> 2. **Per-phase vs Line values:** The KTU answer key always expects per-phase computations first, then multiplication by $\sqrt{3}$ only when asked for line values. Mixing them up costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Units of Φ:** Flux is in **Wb**, not "lines" — never write 0.05 × 10⁸ (the CGS unit). Always SI.
> 4. **k_w < 1:** Don't forget both $k_d$ AND $k_p$. Skipping $k_p$ for short-pitched coils is a frequent error.
> 5. **Zₛ dimension:** Always express per-phase. The SCC gives per-phase current (since ammeters read line current and $I_{sc(phase)} = I_{sc(line)}$ for star).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ☐ **EMF Equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, T_{ph} \, k_w$ — apply per phase; multiply by √3 for line.
- ☐ **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — for 50 Hz, P=2 → 3000 rpm, P=4 → 1500 rpm, P=10 → 600 rpm.
- ☐ **Synchronous Impedance:** $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ — fictitious lumped parameter that *replaces* both leakage and armature reaction effects.
- ☐ **Z_s from OCC/SCC:** $Z_s = E_{0(open)} / I_{sc}$ at same field current — always per phase.
- ☐ **Armature Reaction Effect:**
  - Lagging pf → **demagnetising + cross-magnetising** → flux weakened
  - Unity pf → purely **cross-magnetising** → only distortion, no weakening
  - Leading pf → **magnetising + cross-magnetising** → flux strengthened
- ☐ **Phasor Equation:** $E_0 = V + I_a (R_a + jX_s)$ — KTU's most-tested equation.
- ☐ **Voltage Regulation Formula:** $\%VR = \dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert - \vert V \vert}{\vert V \vert} \times 100$ — positive at lagging, can be negative at leading pf.
- ☐ **EMF Method** (a.k.a. Synchronous Impedance Method) overestimates regulation because it uses unsaturated $Z_s$ — KTU accepts this pessimistic estimate.
- ☐ **Short Circuit Ratio (SCR):** Inverse measure of synchronous reactance; high SCR → good voltage regulation, low SCR → poor regulation but cheaper machine.
- ☐ **Numerical Workflow:** (a) per-phase V and I, (b) phasor decomposition, (c) $E_0$ magnitude, (d) regulation %.
- ☐ **Lagging vs Leading:** Demagnetising vs magnetising; weaker vs stronger E₀; higher vs lower regulation.
- ☐ **SCC is linear** (unsaturated region); OCC is non-linear due to magnetic saturation — this distinction is why the EMF method is approximate.
- ☐ **Cylindrical Rotor = Turbo-Alternator** (1500/3000 rpm, 2/4 poles); **Salient Pole = Hydro-Alternator** (low speed, many poles — Module 2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Regulation - OC and SC Tests - EMF and MMF Methods - ZPF Test - Potier Method - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET402 - SYNCHRONOUS & INDUCTION MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Regulation - OC and SC Tests - EMF and MMF Methods - ZPF Test - Potier Method - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Voltage Regulation — The Operational Heartbeat of a Synchronous Generator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Voltage Regulation (VR)** of a synchronous generator is defined as the **rise in terminal voltage when the load is removed at a given load and power factor, with field excitation and speed held constant**, expressed as a percentage of the rated terminal voltage.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

where:
- $E_0$ = No-load generated EMF (internal EMF, per phase)
- $V$ = Rated terminal voltage (per phase)

**Sign Convention used in KTU examinations:**
- For **lagging power factor** (inductive load): $E_0 > V$, so $VR$ is **positive** (voltage rises on load removal).
- For **leading power factor** (capacitive load): $E_0 < V$, so $VR$ is **negative** (voltage drops on load removal — called the **Ferranti Effect of synchronous machines**).
- For **unity power factor**: $VR$ is small and positive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Voltage regulation tells us how "stiff" a generator is. A small VR means the generator maintains a nearly constant terminal voltage regardless of load. Modern alternators typically have VR between **40–60%** at full-load 0.8 p.f. lagging, but **sophisticated automatic voltage regulators (AVRs)** are used in practice to keep the terminal voltage constant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a water pump (the alternator) connected to a pipeline system (the load). The **pressure at the tap** is the terminal voltage $V$, while the **pressure inside the pump** is the internal EMF $E_0$.

- When you close all taps (no load), the pressure at the tap equals the pump pressure: $E_0 = V$, so $VR = 0\%$.
- When you open a tap (apply load), friction losses inside the pipeline (armature reaction + resistance + leakage reactance) cause the tap pressure to drop.
- The **difference between pump pressure and tap pressure**, when expressed as a percentage of the tap pressure, is the **voltage regulation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Check:** A high voltage regulation means the generator is "soft" — its terminal voltage swings widely between no-load and full-load. A low voltage regulation means the generator is "stiff" — the terminal voltage is stable. This is why **hydroelectric generators (slow-speed, large poles)** typically have lower VR than **turbo-alternators (2-pole, 3000 RPM)**.

### 1.3 The Three Classical Methods of Determining Voltage Regulation

Since $E_0$ cannot be measured directly under loaded conditions without actually loading the machine (which is impractical for large alternators rated 100 MVA or more), three indirect methods are used. All three are derived from the **Phasor Diagram of a Synchronous Generator**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite — The Three Methods:**
> 1. **EMF Method** (also called **Synchronous Impedance Method** by **Maxwell** and **Behn-Eschenburg**) — uses OC and SC tests.
> 2. **MMF Method** (also called **Ampere-Turn Method**) — uses OCC and SCC.
> 3. **Potier Method** (also called **ZPF Method**) — uses OCC, SCC, and ZPF test data.

Each method gives a different value of regulation, and the **true value lies between the EMF method (which gives an upper limit/optimistic value for lagging p.f.) and the MMF method (which gives a lower limit/pessimistic value for lagging p.f.)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **EMF method result as "the most accurate"** — it is NOT! The EMF method is a **pessimistic method for leading p.f. and optimistic for lagging p.f.** The MMF method is the opposite. The **Potier method is the most accurate** of the three classical methods because it accounts for armature reaction by direct measurement (ZPF test) and also accounts for saturation.

### 1.4 OC Test and SC Test — The Foundation of All Classical Methods

**Open Circuit (OC) Test** (also called **No-Load Test**):
- The alternator is run at **synchronous speed** (e.g., 1500 RPM for 50 Hz, 4-pole machine).
- The armature terminals are kept **open-circuited** (no load connected).
- The **field current $I_f$ is varied from zero to a value that gives about **125% of rated voltage** on the armature.
- The **open-circuit terminal voltage $V_{oc}$** is recorded for each value of $I_f$.
- A plot of $V_{oc}$ vs $I_f$ gives the **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — a curve that is initially linear (air-gap line) and then saturates due to iron saturation.

**Short Circuit (SC) Test** (also called **Short-Circuit Test**):
- The armature terminals are **short-circuited through ammeters** (NOT directly — typically through low-resistance shunts or links).
- The alternator is run at **synchronous speed**.
- The field current $I_f$ is increased from zero until the armature current reaches **125% to 150% of the rated current** (for safety, rated current is the safe limit).
- A plot of $I_{sc}$ (armature short-circuit current) vs $I_f$ gives the **Short Circuit Characteristic (SCC)** — a **straight line** passing through the origin (because the short-circuit current is limited mainly by synchronous impedance, and the iron is unsaturated due to heavy armature reaction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is SCC a straight line?**
> Under short-circuit conditions, the armature reaction is heavily **demagnetizing** (especially for a salient-pole machine), which keeps the operating flux density very low — in the unsaturated linear region of the magnetization curve. Hence, $I_{sc} \propto I_f$ linearly.

### 1.5 Short Circuit Ratio (SCR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):**
> **SCR** is defined as the **ratio of the field current required to produce rated voltage on open circuit to the field current required to circulate rated armature current on short circuit**, both at synchronous speed.

$$SCR = \frac{I_f \text{ (for rated } V_{oc})}{I_f \text{ (for rated } I_{sc})}$$

- SCR is also expressed as: $SCR = \dfrac{1}{X_{s(p.u.)}}$ approximately.
- **High SCR (e.g., 1.0–1.5)** → strong machine → better voltage regulation → better parallel operation stability → used for **hydro-generators**.
- **Low SCR (e.g., 0.5–0.7)** → weak machine → cheaper → more compact → used for **turbo-alternators** where stability margin is supplied by fast exciters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phasor Diagram — The Backbone of All Regulation Methods

The phasor diagram of a cylindrical rotor synchronous generator operating at lagging power factor $\cos\phi$ is the foundation. Taking $V$ as the reference phasor along the x-axis:

$$\vec{E_0} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a R_a} + j\vec{I_a X_s}$$

For a per-phase analysis:
- $\vec{V}$ = Terminal voltage per phase (reference)
- $\vec{I_a}$ = Armature current (lags $V$ by $\phi$ for lagging p.f.)
- $\vec{I_a R_a}$ = Resistance drop (in phase with $I_a$)
- $j\vec{I_a X_s}$ = Synchronous reactance drop (leads $I_a$ by 90°)
- $\vec{E_0}$ = No-load EMF, leads $V$ by angle $\delta$ (the load angle / power angle)

> [!NOTE]
> The **load angle $\delta$** is the angle between $E_0$ and $V$. For a generator, $\delta > 0$ (E leads V). For a motor, $\delta < 0$ (E lags V).

### 2.2 EMF Method (Synchronous Impedance Method) — Detailed Theory

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **From OC and SC tests**, compute the **Synchronous Impedance** $Z_s$ per phase:
$$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc} \text{ (phase) at a given } I_f}{I_{sc} \text{ (phase) at the same } I_f}$$

2. With $R_a$ (measured or given), compute the **Synchronous Reactance**:
$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> In many KTU problems, $R_a$ is so small compared to $X_s$ that $X_s \approx Z_s$. Always check the problem data — if $R_a$ is not given, the problem likely assumes $X_s = Z_s$.

3. For a given load condition ($V$, $I_a$, $\cos\phi$), compute $E_0$ from the phasor equation:

$$E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

4. Voltage regulation:
$$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

**Why is EMF Method called "Pessimistic for Lagging p.f."?**
- It assumes $X_s$ is **constant** (independent of saturation).
- It calculates $E_0$ assuming the field current must produce this full EMF on the **saturated OCC**.
- In reality, due to saturation, a smaller field current (and hence a smaller $E_0$) is needed.
- So the EMF method **overestimates $E_0$** and hence **overestimates VR for lagging p.f.** (gives an **upper bound / pessimistic** value).
- For leading p.f., it gives a **lower bound / optimistic** value.

### 2.3 MMF Method (Ampere-Turn Method) — Detailed Theory

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. From OC test, we have the **OCC curve** plotting $V_{oc}$ vs $I_f$ (which represents the relationship between EMF and field MMF).
2. From SC test, the SCC gives $I_{sc}$ vs $I_f$.
3. For the operating condition, compute the **armature reaction MMF** in terms of equivalent field current:
$$I_{fa} = I_f \text{ required to circulate rated } I_a \text{ on SC} = I_{f(SC, rated I_a)}$$

4. The no-load EMF equivalent field current $I_{f0}$ is read from OCC at voltage $V$.
5. The phasor addition of MMFs (treating $I_{f0}$ and $I_{fa}$ as MMF phasors, just like EMF and current phasors) gives the **total field current $I_{fr}$**:

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{fa} \sin\phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The angle of $I_{fa}$ with respect to $I_{f0}$ depends on the **power factor**:
> - **Lagging p.f.**: $I_{fa}$ leads $I_{f0}$ by 90° (demagnetizing — perpendicular in lagging case)
> - **Leading p.f.**: $I_{fa}$ lags $I_{f0}$ by 90°
> - The general relation uses $\sin\phi$ with the appropriate sign.

6. The corresponding no-load EMF $E_0$ is then read from the **OCC** at field current $I_{fr}$.
7. Voltage regulation is computed normally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is MMF Method called "Optimistic for Lagging p.f."?**
> - It correctly accounts for saturation by reading $E_0$ from the actual OCC.
> - But it assumes $X_s$ is negligible and uses pure MMF (magnetizing current) addition.
> - The result is that $E_0$ is **underestimated** for lagging p.f. (gives a **lower bound / optimistic** value).
> - For leading p.f., it gives a **pessimistic (upper bound)** value.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU Board Exam 2024. Every formula here is directly testable.**

| **#** | **Quantity / Method** | **Formula** | **Sign / Note** |
|:-----:|:----------------------|:------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Voltage Regulation | $VR\% = \dfrac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$ | Use per-phase $E_0$ and $V$ |
| 2 | Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s = \dfrac{V_{oc(\text{phase})}}{I_{sc(\text{phase})}}$ | At same $I_f$ on OCC and SCC |
| 3 | Synchronous Reactance | $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ | If $R_a$ given |
| 4 | SCR | $SCR = \dfrac{I_{f,oc(\text{rated } V)}}{I_{f,sc(\text{rated } I_a)}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | EMF Method (Lag p.f.) | $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ | Both terms positive |
| 6 | EMF Method (Lead p.f.) | $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$ | Reactance term subtractive |
| 7 | EMF Method (Unity p.f.) | $E_0 = \sqrt{(V + I_a R_a)^2 + (I_a X_s)^2}$ | $\sin\phi = 0$ |
| 8 | Armature Reaction MMF (as field current) | $I_{fa} = I_{f,sc} \text{ at rated } I_a$ | Read from SCC |
| 9 | MMF Method (Lag p.f.) | $I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{fa}\sin\phi}$ | $+$ sign for lag |
| 10 | MMF Method (Lead p.f.) | $I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 - 2 I_{f0} I_{fa}\sin\phi}$ | $-$ sign for lead |
| 11 | Potier Reactance | $X_p = \dfrac{V_p}{I_a}$ (per phase) | From ZPF test |
| 12 | Potier Triangle Construction | $V_p$ = back-extrapolation of air-gap line | The horizontal shift is $I_{f\phi}$ |
| 13 | True $E_0$ (Potier) | $E_0$ read from OCC at $(I_{f0} + I_{f\phi})$ | Corrected for saturation |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Table Pipe Rule** — In the above table, vertical bars are NOT used. In your handwritten notes, always use absolute value notation properly, but in KTU answer sheets, write equations in plain notation like $\vert V \vert$ or $\mid V \mid$ to avoid ambiguity.

### 2.5 Zero Power Factor (ZPF) Test — The Potier Triangle Foundation

**Purpose:** The ZPF test provides the data needed to construct the **Potier Triangle** and find the **Potier Reactance $X_p$**, which is the **unsaturated leakage reactance** of the armature.

**Test Setup:**
- The alternator is loaded with a **purely inductive load** (e.g., an underexcited synchronous motor or a reactor bank) such that the load power factor is **as close to zero lagging as possible** (typically $\cos\phi \le 0.1$).
- For a constant armature current $I_a$ (e.g., rated $I_a$), the **field current is varied**, and the corresponding terminal voltage $V$ is recorded.
- This gives the **ZPF characteristic curve** — a curve that is approximately **parallel to the OCC (air-gap line)** but shifted.

**Key Construction (Potier Triangle):**
1. Take a point P on the ZPF curve corresponding to **rated voltage $V$ and rated current $I_a$** at ZPF.
2. From P, draw a line **parallel to the air-gap line** (initial linear portion of OCC).
3. The horizontal distance between the OCC and the ZPF curve at the same voltage is the **field current $I_{f\phi}$ required to overcome armature reaction at rated $I_a$**.
4. The vertical distance between the OCC and the ZPF curve at the same field current is the **Potier voltage drop $V_p = I_a X_p$**.
5. The **Potier triangle** is the right-angled triangle with horizontal leg $I_{f\phi}$ and vertical leg $V_p$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the ZPF curve parallel to the air-gap line?**
> At ZPF, the armature reaction is purely **demagnetizing** and is **constant** (since $I_a$ is constant). Hence, shifting the OCC by the constant armature reaction MMF $I_{f\phi}$ to the right and by the Potier drop $I_a X_p$ downward gives the ZPF curve. The two curves must be **parallel** because the underlying magnetizing characteristic is the same (air-gap line).

### 2.6 Potier Method (ZPF Method) — The Most Accurate

**Procedure:**
1. From the **ZPF test**, construct the Potier triangle to find $X_p$ and $I_{f\phi}$.
2. Compute the **voltage drop across Potier reactance** at rated current:
$$V_p = I_a X_p \text{ (per phase)}$$
3. The **total field current required to overcome armature reaction** at the load is $I_{f\phi}$ (read from Potier triangle).
4. The **field current $I_{f0}$ required to induce voltage $V$ on no-load** is read from OCC at $V$.
5. The **total field current** is:
$$I_{fr} = \vec{I_{f0}} + \vec{I_{f\phi}}$$

where the phasor addition is:
$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{f\phi}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{f\phi} \sin\phi}$$

6. The **true no-load EMF $E_0$** is read from the OCC at field current $I_{fr}$.
7. Regulation is computed from $E_0$ and $V$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Potier method is the **industry-standard classical method** because:
> - It uses the **true leakage reactance** (not the synchronous reactance, which includes fictitious armature reaction effects).
> - It accounts for **saturation** through direct OCC reading.
> - It accounts for **armature reaction** through ZPF measurement.
> - It gives regulation values within 2–5% of the actual loaded test, which is the **best** among all three classical methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of EMF Method Formula (EMF Phasor Triangle)

Starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit of a cylindrical rotor synchronous generator:

$$\vec{E_0} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a R_a} + j\vec{I_a X_s}$$

Let us take $\vec{V} = V \angle 0°$ and $\vec{I_a} = I_a \angle -\phi$ (lagging p.f., so current lags voltage by $\phi$).

The phasor $\vec{E_0}$ can be computed by adding the voltage drops. From the geometry of the phasor diagram, the perpendicular projection of $\vec{E_0}$ onto the voltage axis gives:

$$E_0 \cos\delta = V \cos\phi + I_a R_a$$

And the perpendicular component (perpendicular to $\vec{V}$) gives:

$$E_0 \sin\delta = V \sin\phi + I_a X_s$$

Squaring and adding both equations:

$$E_0^2 = (V \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V \sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2$$

Taking the square root gives the EMF method formula for lagging p.f.:

$$E_0 = \sqrt{(V \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V \sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For leading p.f.** (current leads voltage by $\phi$, i.e., $\vec{I_a} = I_a \angle +\phi$):
> The phasor $j\vec{I_a X_s}$ rotates in the opposite sense, so the sign of the reactance term reverses:
> $$E_0 = \sqrt{(V \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V \sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of MMF Method Formula (MMF Phasor Triangle)

The MMF method works on the principle that **field current is proportional to MMF** (since $N_f$ is constant). Thus, instead of adding EMF phasors, we add **MMF phasors** (or equivalent field current phasors).

From the phasor diagram, the total field current $I_{fr}$ required to produce $E_0$ is the **phasor sum** of:
- $I_{f0}$: the field current required to produce terminal voltage $V$ on no-load (read from OCC at $V$).
- $I_{fa}$: the field current required to overcome armature reaction at the given load current (read from SCC at rated $I_a$).

The phasor angle between $I_{f0}$ and $I_{fa}$ is **90° + $\phi$** for lagging p.f. (or **90° - $\phi$** for leading p.f.). Applying the cosine rule to the MMF triangle:

$$I_{fr}^2 = I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{fa} \cos(90° - \phi)$$

Since $\cos(90° - \phi) = \sin\phi$:

$$I_{fr}^2 = I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{fa} \sin\phi$$

For **leading p.f.**, the cosine becomes $\cos(90° + \phi) = -\sin\phi$:

$$I_{fr}^2 = I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 - 2 I_{f0} I_{fa} \sin\phi$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "LAG adds, LEAD subtracts" in the cross term for both EMF and MMF methods.

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 — EMF Method (Full 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 11 kV (line-to-line) star-connected alternator has the following OC and SC test data conducted at synchronous speed:
- **OC Test (Line-to-Line):** Field current of 50 A produces 11 kV.
- **SC Test:** Field current of 50 A produces 750 A (line current) on short circuit.
- Armature resistance per phase = **0.5 $\Omega$**.
- Determine the **synchronous impedance, synchronous reactance, percentage synchronous impedance**, and the **voltage regulation at full load 0.8 p.f. lagging** using the **EMF method**.

**Solution (Step-by-Step, Exhaustive):**

**Step 1: Compute phase voltage and phase current.**

Since the machine is star-connected:
$$V_{oc(\text{phase})} = \frac{V_{oc(\text{line})}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} = 6350.85 \text{ V}$$

Full-load current (assuming rated current corresponds to the SC test rated value):
$$I_{a(\text{phase})} = I_{a(\text{line})} = 750 \text{ A}$$

(For star connection, line current = phase current.)

**Step 2: Compute synchronous impedance per phase.**

$$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc(\text{phase})}}{I_{sc(\text{phase})}} = \frac{6350.85}{750} = 8.468 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute synchronous reactance per phase.**

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{8.468^2 - 0.5^2} = \sqrt{71.71 - 0.25} = \sqrt{71.46} = 8.453 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4: Compute percentage synchronous impedance.**

Base impedance per phase:
$$Z_{base} = \frac{V_{phase}}{I_{phase}} = \frac{6350.85}{750} = 8.468 \text{ }\Omega$$

(In this case, base impedance is numerically equal to $Z_s$ because $V_{oc} = 11$ kV and $I_{sc} = 750$ A are at the same field current — a coincidence for this problem.)

$$Z_{s(p.u.)} = \frac{Z_s}{Z_{base}} = \frac{8.468}{8.468} = 1.0 \text{ p.u.}$$

$$Z_{s(\%)} = 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a special case. In general, we compute p.u. impedance as $V_{rated} / (\sqrt{3} \times I_{rated} \times Z_s)$ when rated values differ from the test values.

**Step 5: Compute full-load terminal voltage per phase.**

$$V = \frac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} = 6350.85 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Compute $E_0$ at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging using EMF method.**

Given: $\cos\phi = 0.8$, so $\sin\phi = 0.6$.

$$E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

Compute each term:
- $V \cos\phi = 6350.85 \times 0.8 = 5080.68$ V
- $I_a R_a = 750 \times 0.5 = 375$ V
- $V \cos\phi + I_a R_a = 5080.68 + 375 = 5455.68$ V
- $V \sin\phi = 6350.85 \times 0.6 = 3810.51$ V
- $I_a X_s = 750 \times 8.453 = 6339.75$ V
- $V \sin\phi + I_a X_s = 3810.51 + 6339.75 = 10150.26$ V

$$E_0 = \sqrt{5455.68^2 + 10150.26^2} = \sqrt{29764,440 + 103,027,778} = \sqrt{132,792,218}$$

$$E_0 = 11523.55 \text{ V (per phase)}$$

**Step 7: Compute voltage regulation.**

$$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{11523.55 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100 = \frac{5172.70}{6350.85} \times 100$$

$$VR(\%) = 81.45\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to use **per-phase** values in the EMF formula! Always convert line voltage to phase voltage for star-connected machines: $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$.

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 — MMF Method

**Problem Statement:**
For the same alternator in Problem 1, the following data is given:
- Open circuit characteristic: $V_{oc} = 11$ kV (line) requires $I_f = 50$ A.
- Short circuit characteristic: $I_{sc} = 750$ A requires $I_f = 50$ A.
- The OCC is approximately linear in the operating region with a slope of **220 V/A per phase**.
- Determine the voltage regulation at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging, using the **MMF method**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Read $I_{f0}$ from OCC at rated terminal voltage.**

Since OCC is linear with slope 220 V/A per phase, the field current to produce rated phase voltage is:

$$I_{f0} = \frac{V_{ph}}{\text{slope}} = \frac{6350.85}{220} = 28.87 \text{ A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, OCC is not perfectly linear (it saturates). For MMF method problems, the problem usually provides the OCC graph or a table of values. For this example, we assume linearity for simplicity.

**Step 2: Read $I_{fa}$ from SCC at rated $I_a$.**

$$I_{fa} = I_{f,sc(\text{rated } I_a)} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Compute $I_{fr}$ using MMF formula for lagging p.f.**

Given: $\sin\phi = \sin(\cos^{-1} 0.8) = 0.6$.

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{fa} \sin\phi}$$

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{28.87^2 + 50^2 + 2 \times 28.87 \times 50 \times 0.6}$$

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{833.48 + 2500 + 1732.2} = \sqrt{5065.68} = 71.17 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Read $E_0$ from OCC at $I_{fr} = 71.17$ A.**

Assuming linear OCC:
$$E_{0(\text{phase})} = 71.17 \times 220 = 15657.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Compute voltage regulation.**

$$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{15657.4 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100 = 146.5\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Notice that the MMF method gives a **higher** regulation than EMF method for lagging p.f. in this case because the OCC is linear and the field current addition amplifies the EMF. In saturated OCC, the MMF method actually gives a **lower** regulation. The exact result depends heavily on the shape of the OCC.

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 — Potier Method (Most Important for KTU)

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-phase, star-connected, 6.6 kV (line), 1000 kVA alternator gave the following test results:
- **OCC (line-to-line, in kV):** $I_f = 30$ A → 3.0 kV; $I_f = 60$ A → 5.5 kV; $I_f = 90$ A → 6.6 kV; $I_f = 120$ A → 7.3 kV; $I_f = 150$ A → 7.7 kV.
- **SCC (line current):** $I_f = 30$ A → 250 A; $I_f = 60$ A → 500 A.
- **ZPF Test (rated current, lagging):** $I_f = 90$ A → 3.8 kV; $I_f = 120$ A → 5.5 kV; $I_f = 150$ A → 6.5 kV.
- Armature resistance per phase = **0.3 $\Omega$**.
- Determine: **(i)** Potier reactance $X_p$ and **$I_{f\phi}$**; **(ii)** Voltage regulation at full load 0.8 p.f. lagging using the Potier method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute full-load current.**

$$I_{rated} = \frac{1000 \text{ kVA}}{\sqrt{3} \times 6.6 \text{ kV}} = \frac{1000}{11.43} = 87.5 \text{ A}$$

(Phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 6600 / \sqrt{3} = 3810.5$ V)

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the SCC was taken up to 500 A (which is well above rated), so the linear SCC has slope $500/60 = 8.33$ A per field ampere.

**Step 2: Construct the air-gap line from OCC initial points.**

From OCC: $I_f = 30$ A → 3.0 kV, so initial slope = $3.0/30 = 0.1$ kV/A = 100 V/A per phase (line) — but for the air-gap line in kV (line), slope = **0.1 kV/A**.

Air-gap line: $V_{ag} = 0.1 \times I_f$ (in kV line).

**Step 3: Construct Potier Triangle at rated point (P).**

Rated point on ZPF curve: at $V = 6.6$ kV (rated), $I_f = ?$ — interpolate between (90 A, 3.8 kV) and (120 A, 5.5 kV):
$$I_f = 90 + \frac{(6.6 - 3.8)}{(5.5 - 3.8)} \times 30 = 90 + \frac{2.8}{1.7} \times 30 = 90 + 49.4 = 139.4 \text{ A}$$

So point **P** is at $V = 6.6$ kV, $I_f = 139.4$ A on the ZPF curve.

Now, from P draw a line **parallel to the air-gap line** (slope 0.1 kV/A) until it meets the OCC. Let's denote the meeting point as Q.

The horizontal distance $I_{f\phi} = I_{fQ} - 139.4$ A. The vertical distance is the Potier voltage $V_p$ (in kV line).

To find Q: starting from P (139.4 A, 6.6 kV), the parallel line has equation:
$$V = 0.1 \times (I_f - 139.4) + 6.6 = 0.1 I_f + (6.6 - 13.94) = 0.1 I_f - 7.34$$

Setting this equal to OCC values: at $I_f = 150$ A, $V_{parallel} = 15 - 7.34 = 7.66$ kV, while OCC gives 7.7 kV. Very close! So Q is approximately at $I_{fQ} \approx 148$ A, $V_Q \approx 7.5$ kV.

So $I_{f\phi} = 148 - 139.4 = 8.6$ A (typical small value).

The vertical distance $V_p = 7.5 - 6.6 = 0.9$ kV (line) = 900 V (line).

Per phase: $V_p = 900 / \sqrt{3} = 519.6$ V.

**Step 4: Compute Potier reactance $X_p$.**

$$X_p = \frac{V_{p(\text{phase})}}{I_{a(\text{phase})}} = \frac{519.6}{87.5} = 5.94 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5: Compute the field current required to produce $V$ on no-load (i.e., $V = 6.6$ kV line).**

From OCC: at $V = 6.6$ kV, $I_f = 90$ A. So $I_{f0} = 90$ A.

**Step 6: Compute the total field current $I_{fr}$.**

For 0.8 p.f. lagging, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:
$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{f\phi}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{f\phi} \sin\phi}$$

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{90^2 + 8.6^2 + 2 \times 90 \times 8.6 \times 0.6}$$

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{8100 + 73.96 + 928.8} = \sqrt{9102.76} = 95.41 \text{ A}$$

**Step 7: Read $E_0$ from OCC at $I_{fr} = 95.41$ A.**

Interpolating OCC between (90 A, 6.6 kV) and (120 A, 7.3 kV):
$$E_0 = 6.6 + \frac{(95.41 - 90)}{(120 - 90)} \times (7.3 - 6.6) = 6.6 + \frac{5.41}{30} \times 0.7 = 6.6 + 0.126 = 6.726 \text{ kV (line)}$$

**Step 8: Compute voltage regulation.**

$$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{6.726 - 6.6}{6.6} \times 100 = \frac{0.126}{6.6} \times 100 = 1.91\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** A regulation of 1.91% is **realistic and low**, which matches the fact that modern alternators are designed to have low regulation. Compare with EMF and MMF methods which would give much higher values for the same machine. This is why the **Potier method is the industry choice** for accurate regulation estimation.

### 3.6 Comparison Matrix — EMF vs MMF vs Potier

| **Parameter** | **EMF Method** | **MMF Method** | **Potier Method** |
|:--------------|:---------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| Tests Required | OC + SC | OC + SC | OC + SC + ZPF |
| Accounts for $R_a$? | Yes | Indirectly | Yes |
| Accounts for $X_l$ (leakage)? | Indirectly (via $X_s$) | No (uses MMF) | Yes (via $X_p$) |
| Accounts for Saturation? | No (uses linear $X_s$) | Yes (via OCC) | Yes (via OCC) |
| Accounts for AR reaction? | Indirectly (via $X_s$) | Yes (via $I_{fa}$) | Yes (via $I_{f\phi}$) |
| Result for Lag p.f. | Pessimistic (overestimate) | Optimistic (underestimate) | Most Accurate |
| Result for Lead p.f. | Optimistic (underestimate) | Pessimistic (overestimate) | Most Accurate |
| True Value Position | Upper Bound (lag) | Lower Bound (lag) | True Value |
| Industry Usage | Educational only | Educational only | Standard Practice |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Voltage Regulation Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Synchronous Generator - KTU 2024] --> B{Choose Method}
    B -->|EMF Method| C[OC and SC Tests]
    B -->|MMF Method| D[OC and SC Tests - Use OCC Graphically]
    B -->|Potier Method| E[OC + SC + ZPF Tests]
    
    C --> F[Compute Zs = Voc Isc]
    F --> G[Compute Xs = sqrt Zs squared minus Ra squared]
    G --> H[Apply EMF Formula with Load V Ia cos phi]
    H --> I[Compute E0]
    I --> J[VR percent = E0 minus V divided by V times 100]
    
    D --> K[Read If0 from OCC at V]
    D --> L[Read Ifa from SCC at rated Ia]
    L --> M[Compute Ifr using MMF Phasor Addition]
    M --> N[Read E0 from OCC at Ifr]
    N --> O[VR percent = E0 minus V divided by V times 100]
    
    E --> P[Construct Potier Triangle from ZPF Data]
    P --> Q[Find Xp and If phi]
    Q --> R[Compute Total Field Current Ifr = If0 plus If phi phasor]
    R --> S[Read True E0 from OCC at Ifr]
    S --> T[VR percent = E0 minus V divided by V times 100]
    
    J --> U[Final Result]
    O --> U
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 EMF Method Phasor Diagram — Cylindrical Rotor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V - Reference Phasor along X axis] --> B[Ia Ra drop - In phase with Ia]
    A --> C[j Ia Xs drop - 90 deg ahead of Ia]
    B --> D[Phasor Sum]
    C --> D
    D --> E[E0 - Leads V by angle delta]
    
    A -.Lagging Power Factor phi.-> F[Ia - Lags V by phi]
    F --> B
    F --> C
```

### 4.3 OC, SC, and ZPF Characteristics — Graphical Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Y_AXIS["Terminal Voltage Voc / Line Current Isc / V_zpf"]
        A1[OCC Curve - Saturates]
        A2[Air Gap Line - Linear Initial Slope]
        A3[SCC Line - Linear From Origin]
        A4[ZPF Curve - Parallel to Air Gap Line]
    end
    
    subgraph X_AXIS["Field Current If"]
        B1[Low If]
        B2[Medium If]
        B3[High If]
    end
    
    A1 -->|Higher in saturated region| B3
    A2 -->|Initial linear part of OCC| B1
    A3 -->|Linear relationship| B1
    A4 -->|Shifted from OCC by If phi| B2
    
    A1 -.Distance Vp Potier Drop.-> A4
    A2 -.Distance If phi Field Current for AR.-> A4
```

### 4.4 Potier Triangle Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with ZPF Curve and OCC Curve] --> B[Locate Point P on ZPF at Rated V and Rated Ia]
    B --> C[Draw Line Through P Parallel to Air Gap Line]
    C --> D[Line Intersects OCC at Point Q]
    D --> E[Horizontal Distance Q minus P = If phi]
    D --> F[Vertical Distance Q minus P = Vp]
    E --> G[Potier Reactance Xp = Vp per phase divided by Ia]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Use Xp and If phi for Voltage Regulation Calculation]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> Define voltage regulation of an alternator. Why is the EMF method called a pessimistic method for lagging power factor loads?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Voltage regulation of an alternator is defined as the **percentage rise in terminal voltage when the full load is removed, with field excitation and speed held constant**, expressed as a percentage of the rated terminal voltage.

$$VR\% = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

**Why EMF Method is Pessimistic (2 marks):**
The EMF method (Synchronous Impedance Method) calculates the synchronous reactance $X_s$ from the OC and SC tests and uses it to compute $E_0$ assuming a **linear** (unsaturated) magnetic circuit. In reality, when the alternator is loaded and the field is adjusted to give the same $E_0$, the magnetic circuit **saturates** less than what the method assumes. Hence the method **overestimates the value of $E_0$** for lagging p.f. loads, which leads to a **higher (pessimistic) value of voltage regulation** than the actual value. Therefore, it gives the **upper bound** of regulation for lagging p.f. loads.

---

**Question A2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
> What is meant by short-circuit ratio (SCR) of a synchronous generator? What is its significance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
SCR is defined as the **ratio of the field current required to generate rated open-circuit voltage to the field current required to circulate rated short-circuit current**, both at synchronous speed.

$$SCR = \frac{I_{f,oc(\text{rated } V)}}{I_{f,sc(\text{rated } I_a)}}$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. SCR is approximately equal to the reciprocal of the per-unit synchronous reactance: $SCR \approx 1/X_{s(p.u.)}$.
2. **High SCR** (e.g., 1.0–1.5) means a strong machine with **better voltage regulation, better stability**, and **higher synchronizing power** — used for **hydro-generators**.
3. **Low SCR** (e.g., 0.5–0.7) means a weak, compact, cheap machine with **poorer regulation** — used for **turbo-alternators** with **fast-acting automatic voltage regulators (AVRs)** to compensate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **any ONE full question out of two choices** from each module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> A 3-phase, star-connected, 11 kV, 5000 kVA alternator has the following test data:
> - OC test (line): 11 kV requires a field current of 80 A.
> - SC test: 200 A requires a field current of 40 A.
> - Armature resistance per phase is 0.4 $\Omega$.
>
> Determine the **percentage regulation at full load 0.8 p.f. lagging** using the **EMF method**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain why the **MMF method is called an optimistic method** for lagging power factor loads. Compare its results with the **EMF method**.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute phase voltage and rated current.**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} = 6350.85 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{rated} = \frac{5000 \text{ kVA}}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \text{ kV}} = \frac{5000}{19.05} = 262.43 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute synchronous impedance at the same field current.**

Since the OCC and SCC data are at different field currents, we scale one of them to a common field current. Let's choose $I_f = 80$ A (since OCC is given at 80 A).

SCC slope: $I_{sc} = 200$ A at $I_f = 40$ A, so $I_{sc}$ at $I_f = 80$ A is:
$$I_{sc(80A)} = 200 \times \frac{80}{40} = 400 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Synchronous impedance per phase:**

$$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc(ph)}}{I_{sc(ph)}} = \frac{6350.85}{400} = 15.877 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4: Synchronous reactance:**

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{15.877^2 - 0.4^2} = \sqrt{252.08 - 0.16} = \sqrt{251.92} = 15.872 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5: Compute $E_0$ at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging.**

For $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:

- $V \cos\phi = 6350.85 \times 0.8 = 5080.68$ V
- $I_a R_a = 262.43 \times 0.4 = 104.97$ V
- $V \cos\phi + I_a R_a = 5185.65$ V
- $V \sin\phi = 6350.85 \times 0.6 = 3810.51$ V
- $I_a X_s = 262.43 \times 15.872 = 4164.85$ V
- $V \sin\phi + I_a X_s = 7975.36$ V

$$E_0 = \sqrt{5185.65^2 + 7975.36^2} = \sqrt{26,890,966 + 63,606,360} = \sqrt{90,497,326}$$

$$E_0 = 9513.0 \text{ V (per phase)}$$

**Step 6: Voltage regulation:**

$$VR\% = \frac{9513.0 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100 = \frac{3162.15}{6350.85} \times 100 = 49.79\%$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

The MMF method uses the **ampere-turn (or field current) phasor addition** rather than EMF phasor addition. The total field current $I_{fr}$ required to give the same excitation is computed by phasor addition of:
- $I_{f0}$ (field current to produce $V$ on no-load, from OCC)
- $I_{fa}$ (field current to produce armature reaction effect, from SCC at rated $I_a$)

Since the method directly uses the OCC (which is non-linear due to saturation) to read $E_0$, it **correctly accounts for saturation**. However, it **overlooks the armature reaction's component of leakage flux** (which is not part of the main field). As a result, the computed $E_0$ is **smaller** than the actual value for lagging p.f., giving a **lower (optimistic) regulation value**. Hence, the MMF method is the **lower bound** for lagging p.f. The true regulation lies between the EMF method (upper bound) and MMF method (lower bound), and the **Potier method** gives a value closest to the true regulation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> A 3-phase, star-connected, 6.6 kV, 1000 kVA synchronous generator gave the following test data:
> - **OCC (line kV):** $I_f = 50$ A → 4.0 kV; $I_f = 100$ A → 6.6 kV; $I_f = 150$ A → 7.5 kV.
> - **SCC (line A):** $I_f = 50$ A → 250 A; $I_f = 100$ A → 500 A.
> - **ZPF Test (line kV at rated current, lagging):** $I_f = 100$ A → 3.8 kV; $I_f = 150$ A → 5.5 kV; $I_f = 200$ A → 6.6 kV.
>
> Determine the **Potier reactance $X_p$** and the field current $I_{f\phi}$. Find the **voltage regulation at full load 0.8 p.f. lagging** using the **Potier method**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> Compare the EMF, MMF, and Potier methods of voltage regulation. Which method gives the most accurate result and why?

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1: Find rated current and phase voltage.**

$$I_{rated} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6.6} = 87.5 \text{ A (line = phase for star)}$$

$$V_{ph} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Air-gap line slope from OCC initial points.**

From $I_f = 50$ A → 4.0 kV (line), slope = $4.0/50 = 0.08$ kV/A (line).

Air-gap line equation: $V_{ag} = 0.08 \times I_f$ (kV line).

**Step 3: Locate Point P on ZPF curve at rated V = 6.6 kV.**

Interpolating ZPF: between (150 A, 5.5 kV) and (200 A, 6.6 kV):
$$I_{f,P} = 150 + \frac{(6.6 - 5.5)}{(6.6 - 5.5)} \times (200 - 150) = 150 + 1 \times 50 = 200 \text{ A}$$

So P is at (200 A, 6.6 kV).

**Step 4: From P, draw line parallel to air-gap line (slope 0.08 kV/A).**

The parallel line has equation: $V = 0.08(I_f - 200) + 6.6 = 0.08 I_f - 16 + 6.6 = 0.08 I_f - 9.4$.

**Step 5: Find intersection Q with OCC.**

Try $I_f = 220$ A: $V_{parallel} = 0.08 \times 220 - 9.4 = 17.6 - 9.4 = 8.2$ kV.

OCC at $I_f = 150$ A is 7.5 kV. Extrapolating OCC (saturating): assume $I_f = 200$ A → 7.9 kV. Setting $V_{parallel} = 7.9$:
$$7.9 = 0.08 I_f - 9.4 \Rightarrow I_f = (7.9 + 9.4)/0.08 = 17.3/0.08 = 216.25 \text{ A}$$

So Q ≈ (216 A, 7.9 kV).

**Step 6: Compute Potier triangle dimensions.**

- $I_{f\phi} = I_{fQ} - I_{fP} = 216 - 200 = 16$ A
- $V_p$ (line) = $V_Q - V_P = 7.9 - 6.6 = 1.3$ kV = 1300 V (line)
- $V_p$ (phase) = $1300/\sqrt{3} = 750.6$ V

**Step 7: Potier reactance.**

$$X_p = \frac{V_{p(\text{phase})}}{I_{rated}} = \frac{750.6}{87.5} = 8.58 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 8: Field current $I_{f0}$ at rated V from OCC.**

From OCC: at $V = 6.6$ kV, $I_f = 100$ A. So $I_{f0} = 100$ A.

**Step 9: Total field current $I_{fr}$ for 0.8 p.f. lag ($\sin\phi = 0.6$):**

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{f\phi}^2 + 2 I_{f0} I_{f\phi} \sin\phi}$$

$$I_{fr} = \sqrt{100^2 + 16^2 + 2 \times 100 \times 16 \times 0.6} = \sqrt{10000 + 256 + 1920} = \sqrt{12176}$$

$$I_{fr} = 110.34 \text{ A}$$

**Step 10: Read $E_0$ from OCC at $I_{fr} = 110.34$ A.**

Interpolating OCC between (100 A, 6.6 kV) and (150 A, 7.5 kV):
$$E_0 = 6.6 + \frac{(110.34 - 100)}{(150 - 100)} \times (7.5 - 6.6) = 6.6 + \frac{10.34}{50} \times 0.9 = 6.6 + 0.186 = 6.786 \text{ kV (line)}$$

**Step 11: Voltage regulation:**

$$VR\% = \frac{6.786 - 6.6}{6.6} \times 100 = 2.82\%$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

A detailed comparison was provided in Section 3.6 of these notes. The **Potier method** is the most accurate because it **directly measures the armature reaction** (via the ZPF test) and **separates the leakage reactance drop** from the armature reaction effect, while also **accounting for saturation** through the OCC.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always convert line values to phase values** for star-connected machines in the EMF method formula. Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ is the **#1 reason students lose 2-3 marks** in regulation problems.
> 2. **For delta-connected machines**, $V_{ph} = V_L$ and $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$. The problem statement will specify the connection — read carefully!
> 3. **For the MMF method**, do NOT use the EMF formula. Use the field current formula with $\sin\phi$ in the cross term.
> 4. **For the Potier method**, remember the **two-step phasor addition**: first find $V_p$ and $I_{f\phi}$, then find $I_{fr}$, then read $E_0$ from the OCC.
> 5. **Always specify the units** clearly (V/phase, A/phase, etc.) to avoid partial credit deduction.
> 6. **For leading p.f.** loads, the regulation may be **negative** — this is **not an error**, it's the Ferranti Effect of synchronous machines. Mention it explicitly for full marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Checklist — Module 1 Regulation:**

- **Voltage Regulation** = percentage rise in terminal voltage from full-load to no-load at constant field and speed: $VR\% = (E_0 - V)/V \times 100$.
- **Positive VR** for lagging p.f.; **negative VR** for leading p.f. (Ferranti effect).
- **OC Test** gives OCC ($V_{oc}$ vs $I_f$, saturating curve). **SC Test** gives SCC ($I_{sc}$ vs $I_f$, linear curve).
- **SCC is a straight line** because heavy armature reaction at SC keeps the iron unsaturated.
- **SCR** = $I_{f,OC(\text{rated }V)} / I_{f,SC(\text{rated }I_a)}$; high SCR = strong machine (hydro), low SCR = weak machine (turbo).
- **Synchronous Impedance** $Z_s = V_{oc(\text{phase})} / I_{sc(\text{phase})}$ at the same $I_f$.
- **EMF Method formula (lag p.f.):** $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_aX_s)^2}$.
- **EMF Method formula (lead p.f.):** $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_aX_s)^2}$.
- **EMF method is PESSIMISTIC for lag p.f.** (overestimates $E_0$, gives upper bound) and **OPTIMISTIC for lead p.f.**
- **MMF Method formula (lag):** $I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{fa}^2 + 2I_{f0}I_{fa}\sin\phi}$; read $E_0$ from OCC at $I_{fr}$.
- **MMF Method formula (lead):** same but with minus sign in the cross term.
- **MMF method is OPTIMISTIC for lag p.f.** (underestimates $E_0$, gives lower bound) and **PESSIMISTIC for lead p.f.**
- **ZPF Test**: Load alternator at $\cos\phi \approx 0$ (lagging) with rated current; vary $I_f$ and record $V$. The ZPF curve is **parallel to the air-gap line**, shifted to the right.
- **Potier Triangle**: Draw a line from a point P (on ZPF at rated V, rated Ia) parallel to the air-gap line until it meets OCC at Q. The **horizontal distance** is $I_{f\phi}$ (AR field current) and the **vertical distance** is $V_p$ (Potier voltage drop).
- **Potier Reactance** $X_p = V_{p(\text{phase})} / I_{a(\text{phase})}$.
- **Potier Method total field current**: $I_{fr} = \sqrt{I_{f0}^2 + I_{f\phi}^2 + 2I_{f0}I_{f\phi}\sin\phi}$; read $E_0$ from OCC at $I_{fr}$.
- **Potier method is the MOST ACCURATE** of the three classical methods.
- **True regulation lies between EMF (upper) and MMF (lower) values for lag p.f.**
- **For star connection**: $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_L$.
- **For delta connection**: $V_{ph} = V_L$, $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$.
- **For 3-phase apparent power**: $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$.
- **KTU Mark Distribution Pattern**: Part (a) of 14-mark questions is usually a calculation (numerical or derivation) worth 7 marks; Part (b) is usually a comparison/explanation worth 7 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** The **Potier Method is the most frequently asked 14-mark question** in the regulation topic, often combined with a derivation of the EMF method or a comparison question. Practice drawing the **Potier triangle construction** clearly with proper labels — examiners give full marks for **well-labeled, neat diagrams** with **clear construction lines and step-by-step working**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Equations in Cylindrical-Rotor Type Synchronous Generator - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Power Flow Equations in Cylindrical-Rotor Synchronous Generator

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET402 - SYNCHRONOUS & INDUCTION MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Equations in Cylindrical-Rotor Type Synchronous Generator - Numerical Problems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator** (also called a non-salient pole or round rotor machine) is a synchronous machine in which the rotor is constructed as a smooth, uniform cylinder with distributed field windings, producing a **uniform air-gap reluctance** around the rotor periphery. As a result, the synchronous reactance $X_s$ is **constant** and independent of the rotor's angular position $\theta$.

The **Power Flow Equations** mathematically relate the **real power (P)** and **reactive power (Q)** delivered by the generator to the terminal voltage $V$, the internal generated EMF $E$, the synchronous reactance $X_s$, and the **load angle (power angle) $\delta$**, which is the angle between $E$ and $V$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, the cylindrical rotor assumption allows the use of a **single synchronous reactance $X_s$** instead of separate $X_d$ and $X_q$ components (used in salient-pole machines). This simplification is the foundation of the power flow equations you must master for numerical problems.

### 1.2 The Phasor Diagram Foundation

The phasor diagram of a cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator supplying a lagging power factor load is the master blueprint from which every power equation is derived.

- $\vec{V}$ = Terminal voltage (taken as reference phasor, along real axis)
- $\vec{I}_a$ = Armature current at load power factor angle $\phi$ (lagging)
- $\vec{E}$ = Internal generated EMF (leads $\vec{V}$ by load angle $\delta$)
- $\vec{I}_a R_a$ = Stator (armature) resistance voltage drop, in phase with $\vec{I}_a$
- $j\vec{I}_a X_s$ = Synchronous reactance voltage drop, leading $\vec{I}_a$ by 90°

The fundamental **EMF equation** of the synchronous generator is:

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I}_a(R_a + jX_s)$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pressure" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Water Pipe Analogy):**
> Imagine a water pump (the **internal EMF $E$**) pushing water through a pipe (the **synchronous reactance $X_s$**) to a customer (the **terminal voltage $V$**). The **load angle $\delta$** is the angle at which the pump pushes relative to the load demand. When the customer demands more water (higher real power $P$), the pump must push harder at a greater angle — increasing $\delta$. The **reactive power $Q$** behaves like pressure waves bouncing back through the pipe — if the customer returns pressure (lagging load), $Q$ is positive (generator delivers reactive power). The power equations simply quantify these flows in mathematical terms.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Synchronous Reactance** $X_s$: typically **1.0 to 2.0 pu** for large generators, measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)** in absolute units.
- **Load Angle** $\delta$: typically **15° to 30°** at full load for stable operation, expressed in **electrical degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**.
- **Frequency**: $f = 50$ **Hz** in India (KTU standard).
- **Synchronous Speed**: $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ **rpm**, where $P$ is the number of poles.

### 1.5 Why Cylindrical Rotor? The Key Simplification

| Feature | Cylindrical Rotor | Salient Pole Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| Air-gap reluctance | **Uniform** | Non-uniform (varies with rotor position) |
| Reactance model | **Single $X_s$** | Separate $X_d$, $X_q$ |
| Power equation form | **Simple sinusoidal** | $\frac{EV}{X_d}\sin\delta + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$ |
| KTU Module 2 focus | ✅ **Primary focus** | Module 3 (advanced) |

> [!NOTE]
> The cylindrical rotor assumption **eliminates the $2\delta$ reluctance term**, making the power equation a clean sine function. This is why the cylindrical rotor case is taught first and forms the basis of nearly all KTU numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. Theoretical Foundation: From Phasor Diagram to Power Equations

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Power Flow Equations

**Step 1 — Establish the EMF Equation**

Starting from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the stator circuit:

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I}_a R_a + j\vec{I}_a X_s = \vec{V} + \vec{I}_a(R_a + jX_s)$$

**Step 2 — Define the Phasor Reference Frame**

Choose $\vec{V}$ as the reference phasor (this is standard convention for power flow analysis):

$$\vec{V} = V\angle 0° \quad \text{(in volts)}$$

The armature current for a lagging power factor load:

$$\vec{I}_a = I_a\angle -\phi = I_a(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

The internal EMF leads $\vec{V}$ by the load angle $\delta$:

$$\vec{E} = E\angle\delta = E(\cos\delta + j\sin\delta)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the EMF Equation Using Rectangular Components**

The complex power delivered by the generator is given by:

$$\vec{S} = P + jQ = 3\vec{V}\vec{I}_a^*$$

where $\vec{I}_a^*$ is the complex conjugate of the armature current, and the factor 3 accounts for **all three phases** of the balanced machine.

**Step 4 — Extract Real and Reactive Power**

By taking the real and imaginary parts of the complex power, we obtain the two fundamental power flow equations for a cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator.

### 2.2 The Two Master Power Flow Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CORE KTU FORMULAS (Memorize these for 14-mark problems):**

**Real (Active) Power Delivered:**

$$P = \frac{EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$$

**Reactive Power Delivered:**

$$Q = \frac{EV}{X_s}\cos\delta - \frac{V^2}{X_s}$$

These are the **king and queen** of all synchronous generator numerical problems. Every KTU question on this topic will reduce to manipulating these two equations.

### 2.3 The Approximate (Neglecting $R_a$) Power Angle Equation

In most large synchronous generators, $R_a \ll X_s$ (armature resistance is typically less than **1%** of synchronous reactance). When $R_a$ is neglected:

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + j\vec{I}_a X_s$$

This simplification is almost always applied in KTU problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. The resulting power equations have the clean form shown above.

### 2.4 Condition for Maximum Power Transfer (Stability Limit)

Differentiating the real power equation with respect to $\delta$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{d\delta} = \frac{EV}{X_s}\cos\delta = 0 \implies \delta = 90°$$

**Maximum Real Power:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{EV}{X_s} \quad \text{(occurs at } \delta = 90°\text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse $P_{max}$ with the rated power. $P_{max}$ is the **theoretical steady-state stability limit**. The generator must always operate with $\delta < 90°$ for stable operation. The rated $\delta$ is typically **20° to 30°** for safety margin.

### 2.5 Complete KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **THE FOLLOWING TABLE CONTAINS ALL HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU NUMERICAL PROBLEMS:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I}_a(R_a + jX_s)$ | EMF equation (phasor form) | V (volts) |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{3EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$ | Real power per phase × 3 — **TOTAL** | W (watts) |
| 3 | $P_{per\ phase} = \dfrac{EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$ | Real power per single phase | W (watts) |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{3EV}{X_s}\cos\delta - \dfrac{3V^2}{X_s}$ | Reactive power — **TOTAL** | VAR (volt-ampere reactive) |
| 5 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{3EV}{X_s}$ | Maximum real power (at $\delta=90°$) | W (watts) |
| 6 | $\delta = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{PX_s}{EV}\right)$ | Load angle for given P | degrees or radians |
| 7 | $I_a\cos\phi = \dfrac{E\cos\delta - V}{X_s}$ | In-phase (real) component of $I_a$ | A (amperes) |
| 8 | $I_a\sin\phi = \dfrac{E\sin\delta}{X_s}$ | Quadrature (reactive) component | A (amperes) |
| 9 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{E\cos\delta - V}{X_s I_a}$ | Power factor extraction | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{E\sin\delta}{E\cos\delta - V}$ | Power factor angle (lagging) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\phi = \delta - \alpha$, where $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{V\sin\delta}{E - V\cos\delta}$ | Alternative $\phi$ calculation | degrees |
| 12 | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = 3V I_a$ | Total apparent power | VA |
| 13 | $I_a = \dfrac{\sqrt{P^2+Q^2}}{3V}$ | Line/phase current (star-connected) | A |
| 14 | $E = \sqrt{(V + I_aR_a\cos\phi + I_aX_s\sin\phi)^2 + (I_aX_s\cos\phi - I_aR_a\sin\phi)^2}$ | EMF magnitude (general) | V |
| 15 | $E^2 = (V\cos\delta + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\delta + I_a X_s)^2$ | EMF magnitude (neglecting $R_a$, $E^2 = V^2 + 2V I_a X_s \sin\phi + I_a^2 X_s^2$) | V |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The power flow equations are **not just academic exercises** — they are used every day in:

- **Power system stability studies** — determining if a generator can ride through a fault without losing synchronism.
- **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) tuning** — adjusting field excitation to control $E$ and therefore $Q$.
- **Governor control** — adjusting mechanical input to control $P$ and $\delta$.
- **Power system dispatch** — economic allocation of $P$ and $Q$ among multiple generators in a grid.
- **Renewable energy integration** — grid codes for wind/solar inverters that mimic synchronous generator behavior (grid-forming inverters).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Power Flow Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DERIVATION 1: Real Power Equation $P = \frac{EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$**

We start from the EMF equation neglecting $R_a$ (a standard KTU assumption for cylindrical rotor):

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + j\vec{I}_a X_s$$

Solving for the armature current:

$$j\vec{I}_a X_s = \vec{E} - \vec{V}$$

$$\vec{I}_a = \frac{\vec{E} - \vec{V}}{jX_s}$$

Take the complex conjugate:

$$\vec{I}_a^* = \frac{\vec{E}^* - \vec{V}^*}{-jX_s} = \frac{\vec{V} - \vec{E}^*}{jX_s}$$

Wait — let's redo this carefully. Using $\dfrac{1}{j} = -j$:

$$\vec{I}_a = \frac{\vec{E} - \vec{V}}{jX_s} = \frac{-j(\vec{E} - \vec{V})}{X_s}$$

The complex power per phase delivered by the generator is:

$$\vec{S}_{ph} = \vec{V}\vec{I}_a^*$$

With $\vec{V} = V\angle 0°$ and $\vec{E} = E\angle\delta$:

$$\vec{I}_a = \frac{-j(E\angle\delta - V\angle 0°)}{X_s} = \frac{-j(E\cos\delta + jE\sin\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$\vec{I}_a = \frac{-jE\cos\delta + E\sin\delta + jV}{X_s} = \frac{E\sin\delta + j(V - E\cos\delta)}{X_s}$$

The complex conjugate is:

$$\vec{I}_a^* = \frac{E\sin\delta - j(V - E\cos\delta)}{X_s} = \frac{E\sin\delta + j(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

Complex power per phase:

$$\vec{S}_{ph} = V\angle 0° \cdot \vec{I}_a^* = V \cdot \frac{E\sin\delta + j(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

$$\vec{S}_{ph} = \frac{V E\sin\delta}{X_s} + j\frac{V(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

**Real power per phase:**

$$P_{ph} = \frac{EV\sin\delta}{X_s}$$

**Reactive power per phase:**

$$Q_{ph} = \frac{V(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s} = \frac{EV\cos\delta - V^2}{X_s}$$

For the complete **3-phase machine**, multiply by 3:

$$P_{3\phi} = \frac{3EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$$

$$Q_{3\phi} = \frac{3EV}{X_s}\cos\delta - \frac{3V^2}{X_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Note for KTU:** In problems, if the values of $E$, $V$, $X_s$ are given as **per-phase (line-to-neutral)** values, then the equations give **per-phase** power. If given as **line values** (line-to-line for voltage), the formula must use $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Finding $\delta$ and $Q$ from Given $P$

> **PROBLEM STATEMENT [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]:**
>
> A 3-phase, 50 Hz, cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator has a synchronous reactance of $X_s = 8\ \Omega$ per phase. The generator delivers a real power of **12 kW** to an infinite busbar at a line-to-line voltage of **400 V** with a power factor of **0.8 lagging**. The excitation EMF per phase is $E = 280$ V. Determine:
> (a) The load angle $\delta$ in electrical degrees.
> (b) The reactive power $Q$ delivered to the infinite busbar.
> (c) Verify the maximum power limit of the generator.

### Solution:

**Given Data Extraction:**
- $X_s = 8\ \Omega$ per phase
- $P = 12\ \text{kW} = 12{,}000\ \text{W}$ (total 3-phase)
- $V_L = 400\ \text{V}$, so $V_{ph} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94\ \text{V}$
- $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging
- $E_{ph} = 280\ \text{V}$

**Part (a): Load Angle $\delta$**

Using the real power equation:

$$P = \frac{3EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$$

Solving for $\sin\delta$:

$$\sin\delta = \frac{PX_s}{3EV} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 8}{3 \times 280 \times 230.94}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$3 \times 280 \times 230.94 = 3 \times 64{,}663.2 = 193{,}989.6$$

Computing the numerator:

$$12{,}000 \times 8 = 96{,}000$$

Therefore:

$$\sin\delta = \frac{96{,}000}{193{,}989.6} = 0.4949$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \sin^{-1}(0.4949) = 29.67° \approx 29.7°}$$

**Part (b): Reactive Power $Q$**

Using the reactive power equation:

$$Q = \frac{3EV}{X_s}\cos\delta - \frac{3V^2}{X_s}$$

First, compute $\cos\delta$:

$$\cos\delta = \cos(29.67°) = 0.8689$$

Compute term 1:

$$\frac{3EV}{X_s}\cos\delta = \frac{3 \times 280 \times 230.94}{8} \times 0.8689$$

$$\frac{3 \times 280 \times 230.94}{8} = \frac{193{,}989.6}{8} = 24{,}248.7\ \text{VAR}$$

$$24{,}248.7 \times 0.8689 = 21{,}069.6\ \text{VAR}$$

Compute term 2:

$$\frac{3V^2}{X_s} = \frac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{8} = \frac{3 \times 53{,}333.3}{8} = \frac{160{,}000}{8} = 20{,}000\ \text{VAR}$$

Therefore:

$$Q = 21{,}069.6 - 20{,}000 = 1{,}069.6\ \text{VAR}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.07\ \text{kVAR}\ (\text{lagging})}$$

**Part (c): Maximum Power Limit**

$$P_{max} = \frac{3EV}{X_s} = \frac{3 \times 280 \times 230.94}{8} = 24{,}248.7\ \text{W} \approx 24.25\ \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 24.25\ \text{kW at } \delta = 90°}$$

**Verification of stability margin:**

$$\text{Operating margin} = \frac{P_{max} - P}{P_{max}} \times 100\% = \frac{24.25 - 12}{24.25} \times 100\% = 50.5\%$$

The generator is operating at **~50% of its theoretical stability limit** — a safe operating point.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Finding $E$ and $\delta$ from $V$, $I_a$, and $\cos\phi$

> **PROBLEM STATEMENT [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]:**
>
> A 3-phase, star-connected, cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator with synchronous reactance $X_s = 10\ \Omega$ per phase supplies a balanced load of **$600\ \text{kW}$** at **$3.3\ \text{kV}$ (line)** with a power factor of **0.9 lagging**. The armature resistance is negligible. Find:
> (a) The armature current $I_a$.
> (b) The internal EMF $E$ per phase.
> (c) The load angle $\delta$.
> (d) The reactive power $Q$ delivered.

### Solution:

**Given Data Extraction:**
- Connection: Star ($\Delta$)
- $X_s = 10\ \Omega$ per phase
- $P = 600{,}000\ \text{W}$
- $V_L = 3300\ \text{V}$, so $V_{ph} = \dfrac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} = 1905.26\ \text{V}$
- $\cos\phi = 0.9$ lagging, so $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.9) = 25.84°$
- $R_a = 0$ (neglected)

**Part (a): Armature Current $I_a$**

Total apparent power:

$$S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{600{,}000}{0.9} = 666{,}666.7\ \text{VA}$$

For a star-connected machine, $S = 3V_{ph}I_a$:

$$I_a = \frac{S}{3V_{ph}} = \frac{666{,}666.7}{3 \times 1905.26} = \frac{666{,}666.7}{5715.78}$$

$$\boxed{I_a = 116.62\ \text{A}}$$

**Part (b): Internal EMF $E$ per Phase**

Using the EMF magnitude formula neglecting $R_a$:

$$E^2 = V^2 + 2VI_aX_s\sin\phi + I_a^2 X_s^2$$

Wait — let me derive this carefully. With $R_a = 0$:

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + j\vec{I}_a X_s$$

With $\vec{V} = V\angle 0°$ and $\vec{I}_a = I_a\angle -\phi$:

$$j\vec{I}_a X_s = I_a X_s \angle (90° - \phi) = I_a X_s (\sin\phi + j\cos\phi)$$

Therefore:

$$\vec{E} = V + jI_aX_s\sin\phi + I_aX_s\cos\phi$$

Wait, let me redo this. $j\vec{I}_a X_s = j I_a X_s (\cos\phi - j\sin\phi) = I_aX_s\sin\phi + jI_aX_s\cos\phi$.

Hmm, that's because $j \times e^{-j\phi} = e^{j(90°-\phi)}$. So $\vec{I}_a = I_a e^{-j\phi} = I_a(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$, and:

$$j\vec{I}_a X_s = I_a X_s \cdot j(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi) = I_a X_s (j\cos\phi + \sin\phi)$$

$$j\vec{I}_a X_s = I_a X_s \sin\phi + jI_a X_s \cos\phi$$

So:

$$\vec{E} = V + I_a X_s \sin\phi + jI_a X_s \cos\phi$$

Magnitude squared:

$$E^2 = (V + I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 + (I_a X_s \cos\phi)^2$$

Computing each term:

$$V = 1905.26\ \text{V}$$
$$I_a X_s = 116.62 \times 10 = 1166.2\ \text{V}$$
$$\sin\phi = \sin(25.84°) = 0.4359$$
$$\cos\phi = 0.9$$

$$I_a X_s \sin\phi = 1166.2 \times 0.4359 = 508.4\ \text{V}$$
$$I_a X_s \cos\phi = 1166.2 \times 0.9 = 1049.6\ \text{V}$$

$$E^2 = (1905.26 + 508.4)^2 + (1049.6)^2$$

$$E^2 = (2413.66)^2 + (1049.6)^2$$

$$E^2 = 5{,}825{,}793 + 1{,}101{,}660 = 6{,}927{,}453$$

$$E = \sqrt{6{,}927{,}453} = 2632.0\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2632\ \text{V per phase}}$$

**Part (c): Load Angle $\delta$**

From the rectangular components of $\vec{E}$:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{I_a X_s \cos\phi}{V + I_a X_s \sin\phi} = \frac{1049.6}{2413.66} = 0.4349$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.4349) = 23.52°}$$

**Part (d): Reactive Power $Q$**

$$Q = P\tan\phi = 600{,}000 \times \tan(25.84°) = 600{,}000 \times 0.4843$$

$$\boxed{Q = 290{,}584\ \text{VAR} \approx 290.6\ \text{kVAR (lagging)}}$$

**Verification using the $Q$ formula:**

$$Q = \frac{3EV}{X_s}\cos\delta - \frac{3V^2}{X_s}$$

$$= \frac{3 \times 2632 \times 1905.26}{10}\cos(23.52°) - \frac{3 \times (1905.26)^2}{10}$$

$$= 1{,}504{,}217 \times 0.9171 - 1{,}089{,}188$$

$$= 1{,}379{,}470 - 1{,}089{,}188 = 290{,}282\ \text{VAR} \approx 290.3\ \text{kVAR} ✓$$

The small difference is due to rounding. **Both methods agree.**

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Finding the $V$-curve Concept Numerically

> **PROBLEM STATEMENT [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]:**
>
> A 3-phase, star-connected, cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator with $X_s = 5\ \Omega$ per phase and negligible $R_a$ is connected to infinite busbars at $V_L = 6.6\ \text{kV}$. The generator is delivering a constant real power of $P = 2000\ \text{kW}$ at unity power factor. If the load is suddenly changed to **0.8 power factor lagging** (with $P$ held constant at 2000 kW), find the new value of $E$ per phase.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V_L = 6600\ \text{V}$, $V_{ph} = 6600/\sqrt{3} = 3810.5\ \text{V}$
- $X_s = 5\ \Omega$, $R_a = 0$
- $P = 2{,}000{,}000\ \text{W}$ (constant)
- Case 1: $\cos\phi_1 = 1.0$ (unity)
- Case 2: $\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$ lagging

**Case 1: Unity Power Factor**

Armature current:

$$I_{a1} = \frac{P}{3V_{ph}\cos\phi_1} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{3 \times 3810.5 \times 1.0} = 174.95\ \text{A}$$

For unity power factor, $\phi_1 = 0°$, so $\sin\phi_1 = 0$:

$$E_1 = \sqrt{V^2 + (I_{a1}X_s)^2} = \sqrt{(3810.5)^2 + (174.95 \times 5)^2}$$

$$E_1 = \sqrt{14{,}519{,}910 + 764{,}400} = \sqrt{15{,}284{,}310} = 3909.5\ \text{V}$$

Load angle:

$$\tan\delta_1 = \frac{I_{a1}X_s}{V} = \frac{874.75}{3810.5} = 0.2296 \implies \delta_1 = 12.93°$$

**Case 2: 0.8 Power Factor Lagging**

New armature current:

$$I_{a2} = \frac{P}{3V_{ph}\cos\phi_2} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{3 \times 3810.5 \times 0.8} = 218.69\ \text{A}$$

For 0.8 lagging: $\phi_2 = 36.87°$, $\sin\phi_2 = 0.6$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$

$$I_{a2}X_s = 218.69 \times 5 = 1093.45\ \text{V}$$

$$E_2 = \sqrt{(V + I_{a2}X_s\sin\phi_2)^2 + (I_{a2}X_s\cos\phi_2)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(3810.5 + 1093.45 \times 0.6)^2 + (1093.45 \times 0.8)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(3810.5 + 656.07)^2 + (874.76)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(4466.57)^2 + (874.76)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{19{,}950{,}336 + 765{,}205} = \sqrt{20{,}715{,}541}$$

$$\boxed{E_2 = 4551.4\ \text{V per phase}}$$

**Key Insight:** Notice that $E$ **increased** from 3909.5 V to 4551.4 V when the power factor became more lagging. This is because the generator must supply more reactive power, requiring greater field excitation. This phenomenon is the basis of the famous **V-curve** of synchronous machines.
