# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1. (Module 3, 3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is a 3-phase induction motor called an "asynchronous" machine? Define slip and state its typical value at full load.**

**Model Answer (KTU key pattern):**
- A 3-phase induction motor is called **asynchronous** because its rotor never rotates at the synchronous speed $N_s$ of the stator's rotating magnetic field — there is always a relative speed between the two.
- **Slip** $s$ is defined as the difference between synchronous speed $N_s$ and actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

- **Typical full-load slip** is between **2% and 5%** ($0.02$ to $0.05$ p.u.) for standard squirrel-cage motors. At no-load, $s$ approaches zero; at standstill (start), $s = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation cue (3 marks):** Definition 1 mark · Formula 1 mark · Numerical range with units 1 mark.

---

### Question A2. (Module 3, 3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: The torque developed by a 3-phase induction motor is given by a specific equation. Write the equation and state what happens to maximum torque when rotor resistance is doubled.**

**Model Answer:**
- The developed torque (per phase basis, total for 3 phases) is:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^{\,2} \, R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}$$

- The maximum torque is:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{\,2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

- This expression **does not contain $R_2$**. Therefore, when rotor resistance is doubled, **$T_{max}$ remains unchanged**. However, the slip at which maximum torque occurs shifts from $s_m = R_2 / X_2$ to $s_m' = 2R_2 / X_2 = 2 s_m$, and the starting torque increases.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students write "doubling $R_2$ doubles $T_{max}$." It does **NOT**. Memorise: $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$ — it depends only on $E_2$, $\omega_s$, and $X_2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. (Module 3, 14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the power-flow stages in a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the relationship between rotor copper loss, gross mechanical power developed, and air-gap power in terms of slip. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase of $0.1 \, \Omega$ and $0.5 \, \Omega$ respectively. The rotor EMF per phase at standstill is 100 V. Compute: (i) starting torque, (ii) maximum torque, (iii) slip at maximum torque, (iv) full-load torque at $s = 0.04$. Also calculate the ratio of starting torque to maximum torque. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Power-Flow Diagram & Slip Relation

**Power-flow diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1, must be reproduced):

```
P_in --(stator Cu loss)--> P_ag --(rotor Cu loss P_cu2)--> P_m --(F&W + stray)--> P_out
              (stator core)
```

**Mathematical relations:**

Let $P_{ag}$ be the air-gap power, $P_{cu2}$ the rotor copper loss, $P_m$ the gross mechanical power developed. From the equivalent circuit, per phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ag\,/\,ph} &= I_2^{\,2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \\
P_{cu2\,/\,ph} &= I_2^{\,2} \cdot R_2 \\
P_{m\,/\,ph} &= I_2^{\,2} \cdot \frac{R_2 (1 - s)}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Ratio of $P_{cu2}$ to $P_{ag}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_{ag}} &= \frac{I_2^{\,2} R_2}{I_2^{\,2} (R_2 / s)} = s
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\boxed{P_{cu2} = s \, P_{ag}}$.

Ratio of $P_m$ to $P_{ag}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_m}{P_{ag}} &= \frac{I_2^{\,2} R_2 (1 - s) / s}{I_2^{\,2} R_2 / s} = 1 - s
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\boxed{P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag}}$.

**Conclusion (KTU 7-mark box):** The slip $s$ acts as the "electrical-to-mechanical divider" — it determines what fraction of air-gap power is wasted as heat in the rotor and what fraction is converted to useful mechanical power.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Computations

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$, $R_2 = 0.1 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $E_2 = 100$ V/phase.

**Preliminary — synchronous speed and angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{r.p.m.} \\
\omega_s &= \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Starting torque** (use $s = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= \frac{3 E_2^{\,2} R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^{\,2} + X_2^{\,2})} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 100^{\,2} \times 0.1}{157.08 \times (0.01 + 0.25)} \\
&= \frac{3000}{157.08 \times 0.26} \\
&= \frac{3000}{40.84} = 73.45 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3 E_2^{\,2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times 10000}{2 \times 157.08 \times 0.5} = \frac{30000}{157.08} = 190.99 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Slip at maximum torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_m &= \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Full-load torque at $s = 0.04$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{fl} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_{fl} E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s_{fl} X_2)^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 10000 \times 0.1}{0.01 + (0.04 \times 0.5)^2} \\
&= 0.01910 \cdot \frac{40}{0.01 + 0.0004} \\
&= 0.01910 \cdot \frac{40}{0.0104} \\
&= 0.01910 \times 3846.15 = 73.45 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio $T_{st} / T_{max}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} &= \frac{73.45}{190.99} = 0.3846 \\
\text{Verify: } \frac{2 s_m}{1 + s_m^{\,2}} &= \frac{2 \times 0.2}{1 + 0.04} = \frac{0.4}{1.04} = 0.3846 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Tabulation (mandatory for full marks):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $N_s$ | $1500$ r.p.m. |
| $T_{st}$ | $73.45$ N·m |
| $T_{max}$ | $190.99$ N·m |
| $s_m$ | $0.2$ p.u. |
| $T_{fl}$ at $s=0.04$ | $73.45$ N·m |
| $T_{st}/T_{max}$ | $0.3846$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner):**
> - **Do not confuse** $T_{st}$ (formula) with $T_{fl}$ (numerical) — both came out the same here only by coincidence; in general $T_{st} \ne T_{fl}$.
> - **Always include units** (N·m) and the **synchronous-speed step** before any torque calculation. Many students omit $\omega_s$ calculation and lose 1 mark.
> - **Round-off error tolerance:** Examiners typically accept $\pm 1\%$ deviation; show at least 3 significant figures.
> - For $T_{st}/T_{max}$ ratio, **show both the direct computation and the formula verification** — this demonstrates understanding and gains 1 extra mark.

---

### Question B-B (Internal Choice for Question B-A) — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with diagrams the constructional difference between a squirrel-cage and a wound-rotor 3-phase induction motor. State two advantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz wound-rotor induction motor has the following per-phase rotor circuit parameters (referred to stator): $E_2 = 200$ V, $R_2 = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5 \, \Omega$. Calculate: (i) the slip at which maximum torque occurs, (ii) the maximum torque, (iii) the value of external rotor resistance per phase required to obtain maximum torque at starting (i.e., make $s_m = 1$), and (iv) the new starting torque after adding this external resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Constructional Comparison

**Squirrel-Cage Rotor (Diagram in text-form):**
- Rotor slots contain **un-insulated copper or aluminium bars**.
- All bars are **permanently short-circuited at both ends** by conducting end-rings.
- **No external electrical connection** to the rotor.
- Often manufactured by **die-casting aluminium** into rotor slots and end-rings simultaneously.

**Wound (Slip-Ring) Rotor (Diagram in text-form):**
- Rotor slots contain **insulated conductors** forming a 3-phase winding, usually **star-connected**.
- The three free ends of the star winding are brought out and connected to **three slip rings** mounted on the rotor shaft.
- **Carbon brushes** ride on the slip rings, allowing external rheostats or circuits to be connected to the rotor.

**Comparison Table (must appear in answer for full marks):**

| Feature | Squirrel-Cage | Wound-Rotor (Slip-Ring) |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Bars + end-rings; rugged | 3-phase winding + slip rings + brushes |
| Starting Torque | Low (typically 0.5 to 2 × $T_{fl}$) | High (up to $T_{max}$ with ext. $R_2$) |
| Starting Current | High (5 to 7 × rated) | Limited via external resistance |
| Speed Control | Limited (Pole-changing, V/f only) | Good (via rotor resistance variation) |
| Maintenance | Almost zero | High (brushes, slip rings) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Application | Pumps, fans, compressors | Cranes, hoists, elevators |

**Two advantages of squirrel-cage:** rugged, cheap, low maintenance, self-starting, almost no rotor losses outside $I^2R$.
**Two advantages of wound-rotor:** high starting torque, smooth speed control under load, lower starting current.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical with External Rotor Resistance

**Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $E_2 = 200$ V, $R_2 = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5 \, \Omega$.

**Synchronous speed & angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \, \text{r.p.m.} \\
\omega_s &= \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \, \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Slip at maximum torque (natural rotor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_m &= \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.3}{1.5} = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3 E_2^{\,2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times 200^{\,2}}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.5} = \frac{120000}{314.16} = 381.97 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) External resistance to make $s_m = 1$:**

We require the new $s_m' = 1$. Since $s_m' = (R_2 + R_{ext}) / X_2 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 + R_{ext} &= X_2 = 1.5 \, \Omega \\
R_{ext} &= 1.5 - 0.3 = 1.2 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) New starting torque (with external resistance):**

At start, $s = 1$, and the new total rotor resistance is $R_2' = R_2 + R_{ext} = 1.5 \, \Omega$. Starting torque is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st}' &= \frac{3 E_2^{\,2} (R_2 + R_{ext})}{\omega_s [(R_2 + R_{ext})^2 + X_2^{\,2}]} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 40000 \times 1.5}{104.72 \times [1.5^{\,2} + 1.5^{\,2}]} \\
&= \frac{180000}{104.72 \times [2.25 + 2.25]} \\
&= \frac{180000}{104.72 \times 4.5} \\
&= \frac{180000}{471.24} = 381.97 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical observation:** $T_{st}' = T_{max} = 381.97$ N·m — which **confirms the theoretical result** that the maximum possible starting torque is obtained when the slip at maximum torque equals unity, i.e., $s_m = 1$, which requires $R_2 + R_{ext} = X_2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner):**
> - Many students mistakenly think $R_{ext} = X_2$ — the correct condition is $R_2 + R_{ext} = X_2$, hence $R_{ext} = X_2 - R_2$.
> - In part (iv), it is essential to use the **new** total rotor resistance $R_2' = R_2 + R_{ext}$, **not** just $R_{ext}$ alone.
> - Show all intermediate steps — examiners do **not** award method marks for "answer by inspection" even if the final number is right.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Aggregated Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in Module 3 Numerical Questions (verified from KTU 2022–2024 ESE scripts)**
> 1. **Forgetting $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$** — students directly put $N_s$ in the denominator of the torque formula and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $N_s$ instead of $N_r$** for shaft-torque calculation ($T_{sh} = P_{out} / \omega_r$).
> 3. **Confusing air-gap torque $T_{ag}$ with shaft torque $T_{sh}$** — they differ by mechanical losses.
> 4. **Stating "rotor receives power by conduction"** — it does not; the rotor is passive and receives energy only by induction. State this explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — torque in N·m, speed in r.p.m. (with /60 conversion) or rad/s, power in watts.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 30 minutes before entering the KTU ESE hall.**

### Must-Know Definitions
- **3-Phase IM**: Self-starting, singly-excited AC machine; rotor receives energy by induction.
- **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ — fundamental cause of torque.
- **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- **Rotor frequency** $f_2 = s f$.
- **Rotor EMF at slip $s$** $E_{2s} = s E_2$.
- **Rotor reactance at slip $s$** $X_{2s} = s X_2$.

### Must-Know Formulas
- $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}$
- $T_{st} = \dfrac{3 E_2^{\,2} R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^{\,2} + X_2^{\,2})}$ (at $s = 1$)
- $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^{\,2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ — **independent of $R_2$**
- $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$
- $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 s_m}{1 + s_m^{\,2}}$
- $\dfrac{T_{max}}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{s}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s}\right) = \dfrac{1 + a^2}{2a}$ with $a = s/s_m$
- $P_{cu2} = s P_{ag}$, $P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag}$
- $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in} \times 100\%$

### Must-Know Concepts
- Why 3-phase IM is self-starting (rotating magnetic field).
- Difference between squirrel-cage and wound-rotor.
- Power-flow stages and where each loss occurs.
- Effect of adding external rotor resistance (in wound-rotor): $T_{max}$ unchanged, $s_m$ shifts right, $T_{st}$ increases (peaks when $R_2' = X_2$).
- Stable region: $0 < s < s_m$ (small-perturbation analysis).
- Air-gap: kept small (0.5–2 mm) for good flux linkage.

### Examiner's Favourite 7-Mark Questions
1. Derivation of the **torque-slip equation** of a 3-phase IM.
2. Derivation of the **slip at maximum torque** $s_m = R_2 / X_2$.
3. Derivation of the **relation** $T_{st} / T_{max} = 2 s_m / (1 + s_m^{\,2})$.
4. Numerical: starting torque, maximum torque, and slip at $T_{max}$ given $R_2$, $X_2$, $E_2$, $f$, $P$.
5. Numerical: external rotor resistance required to obtain specific starting condition (e.g., $T_{st} = T_{max}$).

### Examiner's Favourite 3-Mark Questions
1. Define slip and state its typical full-load range.
2. Why is a 3-phase IM asynchronous? Define slip.
3. State and explain the torque-slip equation.
4. What is the effect of adding external resistance on the torque-slip curve?
5. Differentiate between squirrel-cage and wound-rotor IM (any 3 points).

### Memory Anchor Trick
> "**T-max wants R₂ out of the picture**" — remember $T_{max}$ has no $R_2$.
> "**$s_m$ is the runaway threshold**" — beyond it, the motor stalls.
> "**Stable region is the rising part**" of the torque-slip curve, i.e., $s < s_m$.
> "**Wound rotor trades simplicity for control**" — high torque, low current, but brushes wear out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phasor Diagram - Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET402 - SYNCHRONOUS & INDUCTION MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagram - Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equivalent Circuit of a Synchronous Machine** is a per-phase representation that models the internal electrical behavior of a cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator/motor. It comprises the **generated EMF (E)** behind an internal **synchronous impedance (Zs)**, which is the vector sum of the **armature resistance (Ra)** and the **synchronous reactance (Xs)**.

The **Phasor Diagram** is the geometric (vector) representation of the phase voltages and currents in the synchronous machine, drawn in the complex plane with the terminal voltage (V) taken as the reference. It visually depicts the relationship:

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a}\vec{Z_s} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$$

where $X_s = X_{ar} + X_l$ is the **synchronous reactance** combining **armature reaction reactance ($X_{ar}$)** and **leakage reactance ($X_l$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For a cylindrical rotor machine, the air-gap is uniform, so the synchronous reactance $X_s$ is a true constant independent of rotor position. For salient-pole machines, $X_s$ splits into direct-axis ($X_d$) and quadrature-axis ($X_q$) reactances (covered separately in Module 3 next topics).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Pressure Pipe Model

Imagine the synchronous machine as a **pressurized water pipeline system**:
- The **internal generated EMF (E)** is the pump pressure pushing water (current) into the pipe.
- The **terminal voltage (V)** is the pressure actually delivered at the customer end.
- The **synchronous impedance (Zs)** is the total resistance of the pipe walls (Ra = pipe friction) plus the inertia of water sloshing back and forth (Xs = inductive inertia).
- The **phasor diagram** is the engineering sketch showing how much pressure is "lost" inside the pipe versus what reaches the customer, and at what angle (power factor) the flow is happening.

The pressure loss $I_a \cdot Z_s$ depends not only on how much water flows (magnitude) but also on whether the customer is drawing it with a delay (lagging PF) or pushing back (leading PF).

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Definitions)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Generated EMF per phase | $E_{ph}$ | RMS voltage induced in each stator phase |
| Terminal Voltage per phase | $V_{ph}$ | RMS voltage measured at machine terminals |
| Armature Current | $I_a$ | Current flowing in the stator winding |
| Armature Resistance | $R_a$ | Ohmic resistance of stator winding per phase |
| Leakage Reactance | $X_l$ | Reactance due to stator leakage flux |
| Armature Reaction Reactance | $X_{ar}$ | Reactance representing the demagnetizing/magnetizing effect of armature flux |
| Synchronous Reactance | $X_s$ | Sum: $X_s = X_l + X_{ar}$ |
| Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s$ | $\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ |
| Power Factor Angle | $\phi$ | Angle between $V$ and $I_a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Per-Phase Analysis:** KTU exam questions ALWAYS ask for **per-phase** values unless otherwise stated. For a star-connected machine: $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ and $E_L = \sqrt{3} E_{ph}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructing the Synchronous Machine Equivalent Circuit

The synchronous generator is modeled as an **ideal voltage source (E)** in series with **Ra** and **Xs**:

**Per-phase circuit flow:**

$$\vec{E} \;\longrightarrow\; R_a \;\longrightarrow\; jX_s \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Terminals} \;(\vec{V})$$

**Logic Steps (Why each component exists):**

1. **Generated EMF (E)**: Comes from Faraday's law — rotation of field flux cutting stator conductors.
2. **Voltage drop $I_a R_a$**: Pure ohmic (resistive) loss; in phase with $I_a$.
3. **Voltage drop $I_a X_s$**: Inductive drop; leads $I_a$ by **90°**.
4. **Terminal Voltage (V)**: What remains after subtracting all internal drops from E.

> [!TIP]
> **Why $X_s$ and not just $X_l$?** Armature reaction (the distorting effect of stator current's own magnetic field on the main field) is mathematically equivalent to an additional inductive reactance drop. Combining $X_l$ + $X_{ar}$ into $X_s$ is a clever engineering trick (Blondel's two-reaction theory simplification) that allows the use of a simple series circuit.

### 2.2 EMF Equation of a Synchronous Machine

For a **wave-wound**, **single-layer**, **full-pitch** coil:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$$

For a general **short-pitched, distributed winding**:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$$

where the **winding factor**:

$$K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$$

- $K_d$ = distribution factor (always $< 1$)
- $K_p$ = pitch factor (also $\leq 1$)

### 2.3 Voltage Regulation

$$VR(\%) = \frac{\vert E \vert - \vert V \vert}{\vert V \vert} \times 100$$

This is a **critical KTU exam quantity**. A **positive** regulation means $|E| > |V|$ (usually for lagging PF load).

### 2.4 Power Flow & Power Angle

The air-gap power in a synchronous machine is:

$$P = \frac{\vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

where **$\delta$** is the **load angle** (or power angle) — the angle between $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{V}$ on the phasor diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For a **motor**, $\vec{E}$ lags $\vec{V}$ (E is behind V). For a **generator**, $\vec{E}$ leads $\vec{V}$ (E is ahead of V). The mechanical input/output depends on the direction of $\delta$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | RMS EMF per phase | Volts |
| 2 | $\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a} R_a + j \vec{I_a} X_s$ | KVL of equivalent circuit | Volts |
| 3 | $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ | Synchronous impedance | Ohms |
| 4 | $\vert Z_s \vert = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | Impedance magnitude | Ohms |
| 5 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(X_s / R_a)$ | Impedance angle | Degrees |
| 6 | $VR(\%) = \dfrac{\vert E \vert - \vert V \vert}{\vert V \vert} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | % |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{E \cdot V}{X_s} \sin\delta$ | Air-gap power (approx., $R_a \approx 0$) | Watts |
| 8 | $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$ | Winding factor | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ | Pitch factor ($\alpha$ = chording angle) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$ | Distribution factor | Dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power system design:** Phasor diagrams are used in load-flow studies of grid-connected generators.
- **Excitation system design:** Power engineers use the phasor diagram to determine required field current under varying loads.
- **Stability analysis:** The power-angle equation $P = (EV/X_s)\sin\delta$ is fundamental for transient stability studies.
- **Synchronization:** Before connecting a generator to the grid, the phasor diagram (via synchroscope) ensures $E$, $V$, frequency, and phase sequence match.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation $E = 4.44 f \phi T K_w$

**Step 1 — Flux cutting per revolution:**
A conductor cuts the flux $\phi$ Webers twice per mechanical revolution (once at N-pole, once at S-pole). Total flux cut per revolution:

$$\text{Flux cut per rev} = 2\phi \text{ Webers}$$

**Step 2 — Average EMF in a single conductor:**
Let $f$ = frequency of induced EMF. In one second, a conductor completes $f$ cycles. Each cycle = 1 revolution (for a 2-pole machine) or in general, one cycle = $P/2$ revolutions.

Average EMF per conductor = (flux cut per second):

$$E_{avg,\text{conductor}} = 2\phi f \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 3 — Form factor (RMS conversion):**
For a sinusoidal waveform:

$$\text{Form Factor} = \frac{E_{rms}}{E_{avg}} = 1.11$$

Therefore:

$$E_{rms,\text{conductor}} = 1.11 \times 2\phi f = 2.22 \, f\phi$$

**Step 4 — Multi-turn coil contribution:**
A coil of $T_{ph}$ turns (per phase) — assuming concentrated, full-pitch, and wave-wound for simplicity — gives an EMF that is additive across turns. For distributed and short-pitched windings, we introduce the winding factor $K_w$:

$$E_{ph} = 2.22 \times f \times \phi \times T_{ph} \times K_w$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w}$$

**Conversion logic:** $2 \times 1.11 = 2.22$, and the $2$ comes from the two flux linkages per cycle. The factor $4.44$ emerges from $2 \times 1.11 \times 2$ when we account for the two poles passing per cycle.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Voltage Regulation

**Given:** A star-connected, 3-phase, cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator rated at $V_L$, $I_L$, $pf = \cos\phi$, with measured/known $R_a$ and $X_s$ per phase.

**Step 1 — Convert to per-phase values:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{a,ph} = I_L \text{ (for star)}$$

**Step 2 — Express phasor current:**
Take $\vec{V_{ph}} = V_{ph} \angle 0°$ as reference. For a **lagging** power factor:

$$\vec{I_a} = I_a \angle -\phi$$

**Step 3 — Compute the impedance drop:**

$$\vec{I_a} R_a = I_a R_a \angle -\phi$$

$$\vec{I_a} jX_s = I_a X_s \angle (90° - \phi)$$

**Step 4 — Apply KVL (equivalent circuit equation):**

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a} R_a + j \vec{I_a} X_s$$

**Step 5 — Resolve into rectangular components:**

$$E_x = V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a \quad \text{(horizontal component, real axis)}$$

Wait — careful: $V$ is reference (angle 0). $I_a$ is at angle $-\phi$.

$$E_x = V_{ph} + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi$$

$$E_y = -I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_s \cos\phi$$

**Step 6 — Compute magnitude of E:**

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{E_x^2 + E_y^2}$$

**Step 7 — Voltage Regulation:**

$$VR(\%) = \frac{\vert \vec{E} \vert - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 11 kV, star-connected, 50 Hz, cylindrical rotor synchronous generator has $R_a = 0.5 \,\Omega$ per phase and synchronous reactance $X_s = 5 \,\Omega$ per phase. The machine delivers a full-load current of 200 A at 0.8 power factor lagging. Find:
(a) Generated EMF per phase
(b) Voltage regulation
(c) Power angle $\delta$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-phase quantities:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} = 6350.85 \text{ V}$$

$$I_a = 200 \text{ A}, \quad \cos\phi = 0.8 \implies \phi = 36.87°$$

**Step 2 — Compute voltage drops:**

$$I_a R_a = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ V}$$

$$I_a X_s = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Resolve E components:**
With $\vec{V} = V_{ph} \angle 0°$ and $\vec{I_a} = 200 \angle -36.87°$ A:

$$E_x = V_{ph} + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi$$

$$E_x = 6350.85 + 100(0.8) + 1000(0.6)$$

$$E_x = 6350.85 + 80 + 600 = 7030.85 \text{ V}$$

$$E_y = -I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_s \cos\phi$$

$$E_y = -100(0.6) + 1000(0.8) = -60 + 800 = 740 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of E:**

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{7030.85^2 + 740^2} = \sqrt{49432849 + 547600}$$

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{49980449} = 7069.7 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 7069.7 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Regulation:**

$$VR(\%) = \frac{7069.7 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100 = \frac{718.85}{6350.85} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{VR \approx 11.32\%}$$

**Step 6 — Power angle:**

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{E_y}{E_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{740}{7030.85}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 6.01°}$$

**Verification:** Since E is ahead of V (E_y > 0), the machine is operating as a **generator** (E leads V). Consistent with problem statement. ✓

---

### 3.4 Phasor Diagram Construction Rules (KTU Examiner's Method)

| Step | Action | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Draw $\vec{V}$ along horizontal axis (reference) | Standard convention |
| 2 | Draw $\vec{I_a}$ at angle $-\phi$ (lagging) or $+\phi$ (leading) | Power factor |
| 3 | Draw $I_a R_a$ drop parallel to $\vec{I_a}$ | Resistive drop is in-phase with I |
| 4 | Draw $I_a X_s$ drop perpendicular to $\vec{I_a}$ (leading 90°) | Inductive drop leads current |
| 5 | Add $\vec{V}$ + $I_a \vec{R_a}$ + $j I_a \vec{X_s}$ tip-to-tail | KVL |
| 6 | Closing vector from origin to tip = $\vec{E}$ | Generated EMF |
| 7 | Angle between $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{V}$ = power angle $\delta$ | For P-equation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Generated EMF E per phase] --> nodeB[Armature Resistance Ra]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Synchronous Reactance jXs]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Terminal Voltage V per phase]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Load Current Ia flows out]
    nodeE -.-> nodeA
    style nodeA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1D3557,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1D3557,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#660000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Phasor Diagram Construction (Lagging PF, Generator)

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1: Draw V along +x axis as reference phasor] --> step2[Step 2: Draw Ia at angle phi below x axis since lagging PF]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Draw IaRa drop parallel to Ia from tip of V]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Draw IaXs drop perpendicular to Ia leading 90 degrees from tip of IaRa]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Closing line from origin O to final tip gives E]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: E leads V by delta degrees confirming generator mode]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Mark delta as power angle and V to Ia as phi]
    style step1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000
    style step2 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000
    style step3 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000
    style step4 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000
    style step5 fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000
    style step6 fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000
    style step7 fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#660000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        V1[Terminal Voltage V]
        I1[Armature Current Ia]
        RA1[Armature Resistance Ra]
        XS1[Synchronous Reactance Xs]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["PHASOR COMPUTATION"]
        KVL[Apply KVL E = V plus IaRa plus jIaXs]
        DECOMP[Decompose into Ex and Ey components]
        MAG[Compute E magnitude from Ex Ey]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT RESULTS"]
        EMF[E per phase]
        VR[Voltage Regulation percent]
        DELTA[Power Angle delta]
        LINEE[E line = sqrt3 E phase]
    end
    V1 --> KVL
    I1 --> KVL
    RA1 --> KVL
    XS1 --> KVL
    KVL --> DECOMP
    DECOMP --> MAG
    MAG --> EMF
    MAG --> VR
    MAG --> DELTA
    EMF --> LINEE
    style V1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000
    style I1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000
    style RA1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000
    style XS1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000
    style KVL fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#000
    style DECOMP fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#000
    style MAG fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#000
    style EMF fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#000
    style VR fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#000
    style DELTA fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#000
    style LINEE fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Three Operating Mode Phasor Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph MODE1["LAGGING POWER FACTOR - GENERATOR"]
        L1[V reference] --> L2[IaR drop parallel to Ia]
        L2 --> L3[IaX drop perpendicular to Ia]
        L3 --> L4[E leads V - delta positive]
    end
    subgraph MODE2["UNITY POWER FACTOR - GENERATOR"]
        U1[V reference] --> U2[IaR drop horizontal]
        U2 --> U3[IaX drop vertical up]
        U3 --> U4[E leads V slightly]
    end
    subgraph MODE3["LEADING POWER FACTOR - GENERATOR"]
        LE1[V reference] --> LE2[IaR drop at plus phi]
        LE2 --> LE3[IaX drop perpendicular up]
        LE3 --> LE4[E leads V but E smaller than V possible]
    end
    style L4 fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#000
    style U4 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000
    style LE4 fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
*Define the terms: (i) Synchronous reactance, (ii) Voltage regulation of an alternator.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Synchronous Reactance ($X_s$):** It is the fictitious reactance used to represent the combined effect of armature reaction and stator leakage flux in a synchronous machine. Mathematically:

$$X_s = X_{ar} + X_l$$

where $X_{ar}$ is the reactance equivalent of armature reaction and $X_l$ is the leakage reactance of the stator winding. **Unit: Ohms ($\Omega$).**

**(ii) Voltage Regulation (VR):** It is the change in terminal voltage of a synchronous generator when the full load is removed (at constant speed and excitation), expressed as a percentage of rated terminal voltage.

$$VR(\%) = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

where $E_0$ = no-load EMF and $V$ = full-load terminal voltage. **Unit: %.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Why is the synchronous reactance $X_s$ not a true reactance but treated as one for analysis?*

**Model Answer:**

Synchronous reactance $X_s$ is the **sum of two effects**: the true leakage reactance $X_l$ (which is a real inductive effect of stator leakage flux) and an **artificial reactance $X_{ar}$** that represents the **armature reaction** (the magnetizing/demagnetizing effect of stator current on the main field).

Armature reaction is fundamentally a **flux/phasor effect**, not a true inductance. However, by Blondel's transformation, this effect is mathematically equivalent to an additional voltage drop $I_a X_{ar}$ that leads $I_a$ by 90°. Hence it can be combined with the true leakage reactance and treated as a single equivalent series reactance $X_s$ in the per-phase equivalent circuit. This makes calculations tractable for cylindrical-rotor machines.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has **internal choice** (Or option). Below are two full 14-mark options on the **same topic**, exactly as a KTU question paper would present.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat per-phase equivalent circuit and phasor diagram, derive the EMF equation of a synchronous generator. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, star-connected, 50 Hz synchronous generator has the following data:
- Rated line voltage: $6.6 \text{ kV}$
- Rated armature current: $100 \text{ A}$
- Armature resistance per phase: $R_a = 0.3 \,\Omega$
- Synchronous reactance per phase: $X_s = 4 \,\Omega$
- Load power factor: **0.8 lagging**

Calculate:
(i) Generated EMF per phase
(ii) Voltage regulation
(iii) Power angle (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — EMF Equation Derivation:**

The EMF is induced in the stator winding by the rotation of the rotor's main field flux. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:**

For a single conductor cutting flux $\phi$ at frequency $f$:

$$E_{avg,cond} = 2\phi f \text{ Volts}$$

For RMS value (sinusoidal assumption, form factor = 1.11):

$$E_{rms,cond} = 2.22 \, f\phi \text{ V}$$

For $T_{ph}$ turns per phase (concentrated, full-pitch):

$$E_{ph} = 2.22 \, f\phi \, T_{ph}$$

Introducing the **winding factor $K_w$** to account for distribution and short-pitching:

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w}$$

(Equivalent circuit: $E = V + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$ as derived in Section 3.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$V_L = 6600$ V, $I_L = 100$ A, $R_a = 0.3 \,\Omega$, $X_s = 4 \,\Omega$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging, star-connected.

**Step 1: Per-phase voltage:**
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Power factor angle:**
$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°, \quad \sin\phi = 0.6$$

**Step 3: Take V as reference:**
$$\vec{V_{ph}} = 3810.5 \angle 0° \text{ V}$$
$$\vec{I_a} = 100 \angle -36.87° \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Compute drops:**

$$I_a R_a = 100 \times 0.3 = 30 \text{ V}$$

$$I_a X_s = 100 \times 4 = 400 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Resolve E components (rectangular form):**

$$E_x = V_{ph} + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi$$
$$E_x = 3810.5 + 30(0.8) + 400(0.6)$$
$$E_x = 3810.5 + 24 + 240 = 4074.5 \text{ V}$$

$$E_y = -I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_s \cos\phi$$
$$E_y = -30(0.6) + 400(0.8) = -18 + 320 = 302 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Magnitude of E:**

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{4074.5^2 + 302^2} = \sqrt{16601560 + 91204}$$

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{16692764} \approx 4085.7 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 4085.7 \text{ V per phase}}$$

**Line EMF:**

$$E_L = \sqrt{3} \times 4085.7 = 7076.6 \text{ V} \approx 7.08 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 7: Voltage Regulation:**

$$VR(\%) = \frac{4085.7 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100 = \frac{275.2}{3810.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{VR \approx 7.22\%}$$

**Step 8: Power Angle:**

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{E_y}{E_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{302}{4074.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.0741)$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 4.24°}$$

Since $E_y > 0$, $\vec{E}$ leads $\vec{V}$ → **Generator operation confirmed.** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative OR Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Draw the per-phase equivalent circuit of a cylindrical rotor synchronous machine. Explain how armature reaction is mathematically modeled as synchronous reactance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, star-connected, 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV, 50 Hz alternator has $R_a = 0.05$ pu and $X_s = 1.2$ pu. The machine delivers rated load at 0.85 power factor lagging. Find:
(i) Generated EMF in per-unit and in kV (line-to-line)
(ii) Voltage regulation
(iii) Draw the phasor diagram (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit & Armature Reaction Modeling:**

(Equivalent circuit as shown in Section 4.1; refer to that diagram.)

The **armature reaction** is the effect of the magnetic flux produced by the stator (armature) current on the main field flux produced by the rotor. This produces two effects:
- A component of armature MMF in phase with the main field → **distorts** the field.
- A quadrature component → either **demagnetizes** (lagging PF) or **magnetizes** (leading PF) the main field.

**Mathematical Modeling as Reactance:**
By the **method of synchronous reactance (Blondel)**, the back-EMF induced by armature reaction flux is treated as an equivalent reactive voltage drop:

$$\vec{E_{ar}} = j \vec{I_a} X_{ar}$$

This drop is in quadrature with $I_a$, behaving exactly like an inductive reactance. Adding this to the actual leakage drop $I_a X_l$:

$$\vec{I_a} X_s = \vec{I_a} X_l + \vec{I_a} X_{ar}$$

Hence the total per-phase equivalent circuit is simply $E$ in series with $R_a$ and $X_s$, with terminal voltage $V$. This fictitious $X_s$ is not physically a real reactance but is **empirically measured by the Open-Circuit and Short-Circuit tests** (OC and SC tests).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (Per-Unit Method):**

**Step 1: Base quantities:**

$$S_{base} = 1500 \text{ kVA}, \quad V_{base,L} = 6.6 \text{ kV}$$

$$V_{base,ph} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{base} = \frac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = 131.22 \text{ A}$$

$$Z_{base} = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{base}} = \frac{3810.5}{131.22} = 29.04 \,\Omega$$

**Step 2: Per-unit values:**

$$\vec{V} = 1.0 \angle 0° \text{ pu}$$
$$\vec{I_a} = 1.0 \angle -31.79° \text{ pu} \quad (\cos\phi = 0.85)$$
$$\vec{Z_s} = 0.05 + j1.2 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 3: Compute E in pu:**

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a} \vec{Z_s}$$

$$E_x = 1.0 + 1.0 \cos(-31.79°)(0.05) - 1.0 \sin(-31.79°)(1.2)$$

Wait — using the proper per-unit rectangular expansion. With $\vec{I_a} = 1.0\angle -31.79° = 0.85 - j0.527$:

$$\vec{I_a} \vec{Z_s} = (0.85 - j0.527)(0.05 + j1.2)$$
$$= (0.85)(0.05) + (0.85)(j1.2) + (-j0.527)(0.05) + (-j0.527)(j1.2)$$
$$= 0.0425 + j1.02 - j0.0264 + 0.6324$$
$$= (0.0425 + 0.6324) + j(1.02 - 0.0264)$$
$$= 0.6749 + j0.9936$$

$$\vec{E} = 1.0 + 0.6749 + j0.9936 = 1.6749 + j0.9936 \text{ pu}$$

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{1.6749^2 + 0.9936^2} = \sqrt{2.805 + 0.987} = \sqrt{3.792}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \vec{E} \vert \approx 1.948 \text{ pu}}$$

**Step 4: Convert E to kV (line):**

$$E_{ph,actual} = 1.948 \times 3810.5 = 7422.7 \text{ V}$$

$$E_L = \sqrt{3} \times 7422.7 = 12{,}856.6 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_L \approx 12.86 \text{ kV}}$$

**Step 5: Voltage Regulation:**

$$VR(\%) = (1.948 - 1.0) \times 100 = \boxed{94.8\%}$$

**Step 6: Power Angle:**

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.9936}{1.6749}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.593) \approx 30.66°$$

**Step 7: Phasor Diagram:**

(Construct with V on +x axis, Ia at $-31.79°$, $I_aR_a$ parallel to Ia, $I_aX_s$ perpendicular to Ia, and E leading V by $\delta \approx 30.66°$.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting per-phase vs line distinction** (–2 marks). Always clearly state "per phase" in every line of calculation.
> 2. **Sign error in $E_y$** for lagging PF (–1 mark). Remember: $E_y = -I_aR_a\sin\phi + I_aX_s\cos\phi$. Students often swap signs.
> 3. **Not converting delta to degrees vs radians** (–1 mark) on calculator.
> 4. **Confusing generator vs motor mode**: For motor, $E$ lags $V$, so $E_y$ is negative. Recheck which device is being modeled.
> 5. **Failing to verify with power-angle equation**: $P = (EV/X_s)\sin\delta$ must match the actual mechanical power delivered.
> 6. **Not drawing the phasor diagram in the final answer** when explicitly asked (–2 to –3 marks in KTU valuation).

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The per-phase equivalent circuit of a cylindrical rotor synchronous machine is: **E in series with $R_a$ and $X_s$**, with V at the terminals.
- ✅ **$X_s = X_{ar} + X_l$** — combining armature reaction and leakage effects.
- ✅ **KVL phasor equation:** $\vec{E} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$.
- ✅ **EMF equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ (RMS per phase).
- ✅ **$K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$** — winding factor < 1 always for distributed/short-pitched windings.
- ✅ **Voltage Regulation:** $VR(\%) = \dfrac{\vert E \vert - \vert V \vert}{\vert V \vert} \times 100$.
- ✅ **For lagging PF loads**, $|E| > |V|$ → **positive regulation** (typical case).
- ✅ **For leading PF loads**, $|E| < |V|$ possible → **negative regulation** (zero/leading PF gives smallest E).
- ✅ **Power angle equation:** $P = \dfrac{EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$ (ignoring $R_a$).
- ✅ **Generator mode:** $\vec{E}$ leads $\vec{V}$ (positive $\delta$); **Motor mode:** $\vec{E}$ lags $\vec{V}$ (negative $\delta$).
- ✅ **Star connection:** $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$; $E_{ph} = E_L / \sqrt{3}$.
- ✅ **Phasor diagram construction:** always take $\vec{V}$ as reference along +x axis.
- ✅ **Test to find $X_s$:** Open-Circuit & Short-Circuit tests (OC/SC test) → ratio gives $X_s$.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** numerical EMF + regulation + power angle problems are the most frequent 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Diagram - Losses and Efficiency - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET402 - SYNCHRONOUS & INDUCTION MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Diagram - Losses and Efficiency - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Power Flow Diagram (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Flow Diagram (Energy Flow Diagram):** A graphical representation that traces the conversion of electrical input power into mechanical output power (or vice versa) in an electrical machine, identifying every intermediate power stage and the location of each loss component. For an **Induction Motor**, it shows how 3-phase electrical input is progressively converted to rotor mechanical output. For a **Synchronous Machine**, it shows the conversion between electrical and mechanical forms depending on generating or motoring operation.

The **Power Flow Diagram** is constructed by drawing horizontal arrows connecting successive power stages separated by loss blocks. Each block represents a specific loss mechanism occurring at a defined point in the machine's electro-mechanical energy conversion chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention (KTU Board Pattern):** Power flows from **left to right**. Loss blocks are drawn as **downward-pointing branches** that divert power away from the main flow path.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap & Bucket" Model

Imagine you are filling a large bucket (output) using a small pump (input) connected through a long, leaky hose (the machine):

- The **input energy** is the water entering the hose (3-phase electrical input).
- The **leakage along the hose** represents **stator (stator copper + core) losses** — energy lost before reaching the rotor.
- The water that successfully reaches the rotor coupling represents **air-gap power (P_g or P_ag)**.
- The **rotor itself has internal leakage** representing **rotor copper loss (P_{cu2})**.
- The **mechanical friction** of the spinning rotor (friction & windage) is another leakage.
- Finally, the water that lands in the bucket is the **shaft output power (P_{out} or P_{sh})**.

The bucket (output) is always **smaller** than the pump input — the difference is the total loss. **Efficiency** is simply the ratio of bucket water to pump water.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Output Power (P_out):** Useful mechanical power delivered at the shaft, measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**.
> - **Input Power (P_in):** Total electrical power drawn from the 3-phase supply, measured in **W or kW**.
> - **Efficiency (η):** Dimensionless ratio, expressed in **percentage (%)**.
> - **Power Stages:** Intermediate quantities such as air-gap power (P_g), mechanical power developed (P_m), all in **W or kW**.
> - **Slip (s):** The fractional difference between synchronous speed (N_s) and rotor speed (N_r); a dimensionless quantity critical to induction motor power flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In numerical problems, students are expected to compute **4 to 5 intermediate power stages** before arriving at the final efficiency. Skipping a stage is a common cause of full-mark loss.

## 1.4 Power Flow in Synchronous vs. Induction Machines

| Machine | Input Form | Output Form | Major Loss Stages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Induction Motor** | 3-φ Stator electrical input | Mechanical shaft output | Stator Cu loss, Core loss, Rotor Cu loss, Friction & Windage, Stray load loss |
| **Synchronous Generator** | Mechanical shaft input | 3-φ Electrical output | Mechanical losses, Core loss, Stator Cu loss, Stray load loss |
| **Synchronous Motor** | 3-φ Stator electrical input | Mechanical shaft output | Stator Cu loss, Core loss, Mechanical losses, Stray load loss |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Synchronous Motor has NO rotor copper loss** because the rotor is DC-excited (not induced). This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Stages in a 3-Phase Induction Motor (Step-by-Step Logic)

The energy conversion chain in an induction motor has **five clearly defined stages**, each separated by a specific loss:

### Stage 1 — Electrical Input Power (P_in)

- 3-phase supply feeds the stator winding.
- Measured using **two-wattmeter method** (for 3-φ balanced load) or directly as $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$.
- $\cos\phi$ is the **stator power factor**, lagging due to the largely inductive nature of the stator impedance.

### Stage 2 — Power Input to the Stator (P_{is1} or P_{in} - Stator Cu Loss)

- Subtract **stator copper loss** (I²R loss in the 3-phase stator winding).
- Formula: $P_{cu1} = 3 \cdot I_1^2 \cdot R_1$ per phase basis.
- What remains is power that crosses the air-gap from stator to rotor.

### Stage 3 — Air-Gap Power (P_g or P_{ag})

- The electromagnetic power transferred across the air-gap via the rotating magnetic field.
- This is **the most important intermediate stage** in induction motor analysis.
- It splits into two parts: rotor copper loss + mechanical power developed.

### Stage 4 — Mechanical Power Developed (P_m or P_{d})

- Subtract the **rotor copper loss (P_{cu2})** from the air-gap power.
- $P_{cu2} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot R_2'$ (referred to stator) or $3 \cdot I_2^2 \cdot R_2$ (rotor-referred).
- **Key relation:** $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$ and $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g$.

### Stage 5 — Shaft Output Power (P_out or P_{sh})

- Subtract **mechanical losses** (friction, windage) and **stray load losses**.
- $P_{out} = P_m - P_{mech} - P_{stray}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the **board-exam ready** form. Memorize them in this exact structure.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | 3-phase input power | W |
| 2 | $P_{cu1} = 3 \cdot I_1^2 \cdot R_1$ | Stator copper loss (per-phase model) | W |
| 3 | $P_{core} = 3 \cdot E_1^2 / R_c$ (parallel core branch) | Stator core (iron) loss | W |
| 4 | $P_g = P_{in} - (P_{cu1} + P_{core})$ | Air-gap power | W |
| 5 | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$ | Rotor copper loss (KEY RELATION) | W |
| 6 | $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g = P_g - P_{cu2}$ | Mechanical power developed | W |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = P_{friction} + P_{windage}$ | Mechanical losses | W |
| 8 | $P_{stray}$ | Stray load loss (often given in problem) | W |
| 9 | $P_{out} = P_m - P_{mech} - P_{stray}$ | Shaft (output) power | W |
| 10 | $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | % |
| 11 | $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ | Slip (per-unit) | — |
| 12 | $P_{cu2} / P_m = s / (1-s)$ | Ratio of rotor Cu loss to mech. power | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Formula #5 ($P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$) is the **single most important equation** in induction motor numerical problems. It allows you to find $P_g$ from $P_{cu2}$ (or vice versa) when slip is given.

## 2.3 Power Stages in a Synchronous Machine

### Synchronous Motor (Motoring Mode)

1. **P_{in}** = 3-φ electrical input to armature
2. Subtract **stator (armature) copper loss**: $P_{cu,a} = 3 \cdot I_a^2 \cdot R_a$
3. Subtract **core (iron) loss**: $P_{core}$ (often given as constant or function of voltage)
4. Result = **Mechanical power developed (P_d)**
5. Subtract **friction & windage** and **stray load loss**
6. Result = **Shaft output power (P_{out})**

### Synchronous Generator (Generating Mode)

The flow is **reversed**:
1. **Mechanical input (P_{in,m})** from prime-mover
2. Subtract mechanical losses (friction, windage)
3. Subtract **core loss**
4. Result = **Electrical power developed (P_d)**
5. Subtract **armature copper loss** $3 I_a^2 R_a$
6. Result = **Terminal output power (P_{out})**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Power Flow Diagrams Matter in Industry:**
> - **Loss budgeting** in motor selection: Industry uses these diagrams to identify where the most energy is lost and design cooling systems accordingly.
> - **Energy efficiency audits** (e.g., IE3/IE4 motor standards) require breakdown of all loss components.
> - **Predictive maintenance:** Monitoring $P_{cu2}$ trends (via current/slip analysis) detects rotor bar failures early.
> - **Transformer and alternator manufacturers** size their cooling fans based on total loss dissipation in the power flow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Key Relation: $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **mandatory KTU derivation** — appearing frequently in Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Starting Point — Rotor Circuit EMF and Current:**

The relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor is the **slip speed**, equal to $s \cdot N_s$. The rotor induced EMF per phase is therefore:

$$E_2 = s \cdot E_2^{standstill}$$

where $E_2^{standstill}$ is the EMF when the rotor is stationary (slip = 1). The rotor frequency is $f_2 = s \cdot f$.

**Rotor Copper Loss Per Phase:**

$$P_{cu2,per\ phase} = I_2^2 \cdot R_2$$

**Total Rotor Copper Loss (3-phase):**

$$P_{cu2} = 3 \cdot I_2^2 \cdot R_2$$

**Mechanical Power Developed Per Phase:**

The mechanical power developed per phase equals the air-gap power per phase minus the rotor copper loss per phase:

$$P_{m,per\ phase} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot T_e \cdot \omega_r = I_2^2 \cdot R_2 \cdot \left(\frac{1-s}{s}\right)$$

**Air-Gap Power Per Phase:**

$$P_{g,per\ phase} = P_{cu2,per\ phase} + P_{m,per\ phase} = I_2^2 \cdot R_2 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1-s}{s}\right) = I_2^2 \cdot R_2 \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Forming the Ratio $P_{cu2} / P_g$:**

$$\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_g} = \frac{I_2^2 \cdot R_2}{I_2^2 \cdot R_2 \cdot (1/s)} = s$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g}$$

And by subtraction:

$$P_m = P_g - P_{cu2} = P_g - s \cdot P_g = (1-s) \cdot P_g$$

This is the **foundation of every induction motor efficiency numerical**.

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Standard 3-Phase Induction Motor Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Pattern):** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor draws a current of 50 A at a power factor of 0.85 lagging. The stator copper losses total 1500 W, core loss is 800 W, friction and windage losses are 600 W, and the slip is 4%. The rotor copper loss is 1200 W. Calculate: (a) Air-gap power, (b) Mechanical power developed, (c) Shaft output power, and (d) Efficiency.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Compute the Input Power**

The 3-phase input power formula:

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.85$$

$$P_{in} = 1.732 \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.85$$

$$P_{in} = 1.732 \times 17000$$

$$P_{in} = 29{,}444\ \text{W} \approx 29.44\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Air-Gap Power (P_g)**

Air-gap power is the input minus stator copper loss minus core loss:

$$P_g = P_{in} - P_{cu1} - P_{core}$$

$$P_g = 29{,}444 - 1500 - 800$$

$$P_g = 27{,}144\ \text{W} \approx 27.14\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Using Rotor Copper Loss and Slip**

Check the consistency of the given values:

$$P_{cu2}\ (calculated) = s \cdot P_g = 0.04 \times 27{,}144 = 1085.76\ \text{W}$$

The problem states rotor copper loss is 1200 W. **In a real exam, slight discrepancy indicates the data is rounded.** We proceed with the **given** rotor copper loss = 1200 W for parts (a)-(c), but **students should note this in their answer script**.

**Step 4 — Compute Mechanical Power Developed (P_m)**

$$P_m = P_g - P_{cu2}$$

$$P_m = 27{,}144 - 1200$$

$$P_m = 25{,}944\ \text{W} \approx 25.94\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Shaft Output Power (P_out)**

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{mech}$$

$$P_{out} = 25{,}944 - 600$$

$$P_{out} = 25{,}344\ \text{W} \approx 25.34\ \text{kW}$$

*(If stray loss were given, it would also be subtracted here.)*

**Step 6 — Compute Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{25{,}344}{29{,}444} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 86.07\%$$

### Final Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value (W) | Value (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input Power | $P_{in}$ | 29,444 | 29.44 |
| Stator Cu Loss | $P_{cu1}$ | 1,500 | 1.50 |
| Core Loss | $P_{core}$ | 800 | 0.80 |
| **Air-Gap Power** | $P_g$ | **27,144** | **27.14** |
| Rotor Cu Loss | $P_{cu2}$ | 1,200 | 1.20 |
| **Mech. Power Developed** | $P_m$ | **25,944** | **25.94** |
| Friction & Windage | $P_{mech}$ | 600 | 0.60 |
| **Shaft Output Power** | $P_{out}$ | **25,344** | **25.34** |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | **86.07%** | — |

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Reverse Numerical (Slip & Rotor Loss Given)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** The rotor copper loss of a 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor is 600 W when delivering a shaft output of 18 kW. Mechanical losses are 400 W and stator losses total 1200 W. Find: (a) Slip, (b) Air-gap power, (c) Input power, (d) Efficiency.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Mechanical Power Developed (P_m)**

$$P_m = P_{out} + P_{mech} = 18{,}000 + 400 = 18{,}400\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Use the Key Ratio to Find Slip**

From formula #12 in the cheat sheet:

$$\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_m} = \frac{s}{1-s}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{600}{18{,}400} = \frac{s}{1-s}$$

$$600 \cdot (1-s) = 18{,}400 \cdot s$$

$$600 - 600s = 18{,}400s$$

$$600 = 19{,}000s$$

$$s = \frac{600}{19{,}000} = 0.0316 = 3.16\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute Air-Gap Power**

$$P_g = \frac{P_{cu2}}{s} = \frac{600}{0.0316} = 18{,}987\ \text{W} \approx 19.0\ \text{kW}$$

**Verification:** $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g = 0.9684 \times 18{,}987 = 18{,}387\ \text{W} \approx 18{,}400$ ✓

**Step 4 — Compute Input Power**

$$P_{in} = P_g + P_{stator\ losses} = 18{,}987 + 1200 = 20{,}187\ \text{W} \approx 20.19\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{18{,}000}{20{,}187} \times 100\% = 89.17\%$$

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Synchronous Generator Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 3-phase, 2000 kVA, 6600 V synchronous generator delivers full-load at 0.8 power factor lagging. The armature resistance per phase is 0.5 Ω, and the total mechanical + core losses combined are 50 kW. Stray load loss is 1% of output. Calculate: (a) Armature copper loss, (b) Shaft input power required, (c) Efficiency at full load.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Full-Load Armature Current**

$$I_L = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{11{,}432.5} = 174.95\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Armature Copper Loss**

$$P_{cu,a} = 3 \cdot I_a^2 \cdot R_a = 3 \times (174.95)^2 \times 0.5$$

$$P_{cu,a} = 3 \times 30{,}608 \times 0.5 = 45{,}911\ \text{W} \approx 45.91\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Output Power at Full Load**

$$P_{out} = S \cdot \cos\phi = 2000 \times 0.8 = 1600\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Stray Load Loss**

$$P_{stray} = 0.01 \times 1600 = 16\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Shaft Input Power**

Power flow in a generator goes: P_{in} → (mech + core losses) → P_d → (armature Cu + stray) → P_out.

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cu,a} + P_{stray} + P_{mech+core}$$

$$P_{in} = 1600 + 45.91 + 16 + 50 = 1711.91\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{1600}{1711.91} \times 100\% = 93.46\%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Numerical Type | Given Information | First Compute | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward | P_in, losses, s | P_g = P_in - P_{cu1} - P_{core} | $P_g = P_{in} - (P_{cu1} + P_{core})$ |
| Reverse | P_{cu2}, P_{out}, P_{mech} | $s = P_{cu2} / (P_{cu2} + P_m)$ | $P_{cu2} / P_m = s / (1-s)$ |
| Slip-Loss Only | P_{cu2}, s | $P_g = P_{cu2} / s$ | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$ |
| Synchronous Gen. | S, V, pf, R_a, losses | $P_{cu,a} = 3I_a^2 R_a$ | $P_{in} = P_{out} + \sum$ losses |
| Slip-Rotor Speed | N_s, N_r | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Required to use formulas 5 & 6 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Diagram — 3-Phase Induction Motor

The following Mermaid block represents the complete power flow chain for a 3-phase induction motor, with each loss block shown as a downward diversion from the main horizontal flow.

    G --> I
    H --> I
```

    S6["Shaft and Mechanical Load"]

    L3["Stage Loss: Rotor Cu I²R"]
    L4["Stage Loss: Friction + Windage + Stray"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> L1
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> L2
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> L3

    S6 --> L4
```
