> - **Topic:** Braking of Induction Motors - Plugging - Dynamic Braking - Regenerative Braking (Concepts only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Electrical Braking in Induction Motors?

**Electrical Braking** is the technique of rapidly decelerating a three-phase induction motor by deliberately converting its mechanical kinetic energy into electrical energy (which is then dissipated as heat or returned to the supply). Unlike mechanical friction brakes, electrical braking is **wear-free**, **smooth**, and **controllable**, making it indispensable in industrial drives such as cranes, hoists, elevators, locomotives, and machine tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEET402 / Module 4):**
> *Braking of induction motors refers to the methods used to bring the motor to a rapid stop or to control its speed during overhauling loads. The three principal electrical braking methods are Plugging, Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking, and Regenerative Braking.*

The braking torque $T_b$ is the electromagnetic torque developed in the **opposite direction** to the rotation of the rotor. When $T_b$ acts opposite to $T_{load}$, the net torque becomes negative, and the motor decelerates according to:

$$J \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} = T_{load} - T_{motor} - T_b$$

where $J$ is the moment of inertia of the rotor-load system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Braking Required?**
> - To achieve **fast stopping** in emergency or process cycles.
> - To **limit overspeed** during overhauling loads (e.g., a loaded goods lift descending).
> - To **hold a load** against gravity in cranes and hoists.
> - To achieve **controlled deceleration** instead of a long natural coast-down.

---

## 1.2 The Three Core Braking Methods – At a Glance

| Braking Method | Core Idea | Energy Destination | Final Speed | Speed Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plugging** | Reverse any two stator phases | Rotor copper loss (heat) | Zero (must disconnect!) | Above synchronous |
| **Dynamic Braking** | Replace AC with DC in stator | Rotor resistance (heat) | Zero | Below synchronous |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Run as induction generator | Returned to supply | Cannot reach zero | Above synchronous |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *“PDR = Plug, Dissipate, Return.”* Plugging and Dynamic both **dissipate** energy as heat; only Regenerative **returns** energy to the grid.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Bicycle Brake

Imagine you are cycling downhill:

1. **Plugging** is like suddenly **pedalling backwards** while moving forward. The chain fights the wheels, energy is wasted as heat in the chain/gears, and you must release the pedal the moment you stop (otherwise you accelerate backwards).

2. **Dynamic Braking** is like **squeezing the handbrake**. The brake pads convert your kinetic energy into heat at the rim. You stop completely, but all the energy is wasted as heat.

3. **Regenerative Braking** is like **shifting to a lower gear and back-pedalling** such that the wheel drives the chain, charging a battery (the grid). It only works when you are moving faster than the natural "idle speed" of the drivetrain, and it cannot hold you stationary – you still need the handbrake for the last step.

---

## 1.4 The Slip Concept – The Heart of All Braking

Recall that slip $s$ is defined as:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

where $N_s$ = synchronous speed (rev/min) and $N_r$ = actual rotor speed (rev/min).

The torque-slip relationship of an induction motor is:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/s\right)^2 + \left(X_1 + X_2'\right)^2}$$

The **sign of the slip** and the **sign of the torque** determine the operating region:

- $0 < s < 1$ : Motoring (forward torque, forward speed)
- $s \approx 1$ : Starting
- $s > 1$ : Plugging (forward speed, reverse torque)
- $s < 0$ : Regenerative (forward speed, reverse torque)
- $s \approx 0$ : Synchronous (no torque)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All three braking methods essentially exploit different slip regions or different rotor-current mechanisms to produce a reverse (braking) torque.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Method 1: PLUGGING (Reverse Current Braking)

### Operating Principle

In plugging, **any two of the three stator supply leads are interchanged**. This reverses the direction of the rotating magnetic field, so the field now rotates in the opposite direction to the rotor. The rotor, still spinning in its original direction, now sees a relative speed of $(N_s + N_r)$ instead of $(N_s - N_r)$.

### Slip During Plugging

$$s_{plug} = \frac{-N_s - N_r}{-N_s} = \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s}$$

Substituting $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$, we obtain the **famous plug-slip identity**:

$$\boxed{\;s_{plug} = 2 - s_{orig}\;}$$

If the motor was running at a small slip $s = 0.05$, then $s_{plug} = 1.95$, meaning the rotor effectively operates at **nearly double synchronous speed** in the reverse field.

### Braking Torque During Plugging

By substituting $s_{plug} = 2 - s$ in the torque equation, the braking torque at the moment of plugging becomes:

$$T_{b,plug} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/(2-s))}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/(2-s)\right)^2 + \left(X_1 + X_2'\right)^2}$$

This braking torque is **much larger** than the starting torque because $R_2'/(2-s) \gg R_2'$ (since $2-s$ is small for a normally-running motor). Hence the rotor draws a heavy current.

### Disadvantages & Safety

- Huge current surge (often **5 to 7 times** normal current).
- Substantial heat dissipation in the rotor cage – risk of thermal damage.
- **The supply must be disconnected at exactly zero speed**, otherwise the motor will accelerate in the opposite direction.
- An auxiliary **zero-speed switch** or **zero-speed relay** is mandatory.

### Where Plugging is Used

- Printing presses, rolling mills, textile machinery – applications needing **fast reversal**.
- Small motors where the energy per stop is manageable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that during plugging the slip becomes $2s$. The correct expression is $s_{plug} = 2 - s$, **not** $2s$.

---

## 2.2 Method 2: DYNAMIC BRAKING (Rheostatic / DC Braking)

### Operating Principle

In dynamic braking:

1. The **three-phase AC supply is disconnected** from the stator.
2. A **DC voltage** (typically obtained from a rectifier or a DC exciter) is applied across **any two stator terminals**.
3. The DC current produces a **stationary (non-rotating) magnetic field** in the air gap.
4. The rotor, still spinning, **cuts this stationary field** and induces an EMF.
5. The induced rotor current interacts with the stationary field to produce a **braking torque** that opposes rotation (Lenz's Law).

### Equivalent Circuit View

The stationary field is analogous to a transformer with a DC primary. The rotor "sees" a field of constant polarity in space but a constant-amplitude rotating frame in the rotor reference. The effective induced frequency is the slip frequency $f_r = s f$. At normal speeds $s \approx 1$, the rotor frequency is essentially the supply frequency.

### Braking Torque Expression

The dynamic braking torque is given by:

$$T_{db} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{dc,eq}^2 \cdot R_2' / s}{\left(R_1 + R_2' / s\right)^2 + \left(X_1 + X_2'\right)^2}$$

where $V_{dc,eq}$ is the equivalent AC RMS voltage that produces the same air-gap MMF as the DC excitation. For a star-connected stator with DC applied between two lines, the equivalent AC voltage is:

$$V_{dc,eq} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slip here is measured relative to the **stationary field** (which is like a synchronous field of speed zero), so $s = N_r / N_s$ effectively.

### Important Sub-Case: Capacitor Self-Excitation

In some self-excited induction generators used for braking (especially in remote/stand-alone setups), the DC source is replaced by **capacitors** across the stator terminals. The residual magnetism of the rotor builds up the terminal voltage through the capacitors, producing the stationary/rotating field. This is the basis of the **self-excited induction generator** used in wind-power applications.

### Advantages

- No risk of reverse rotation – motor **stops at zero** naturally.
- No AC supply is needed after the initial contactor action.
- Smooth, controllable stop.
- Suitable for **emergency braking**.

### Disadvantages

- Braking torque vanishes as $N_r \to 0$ (no relative motion → no induced EMF → no torque). Hence it cannot **hold** a stationary load.
- Energy is dissipated as heat in the rotor circuit.
- A separate DC source or capacitor bank is required.

### Where Dynamic Braking is Used

- Elevators, cranes, conveyor belts, machine tools, escalators.
- Wherever a fail-safe stop is required without risk of reverse motion.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Dynamic braking converts the **rotor kinetic energy** into **rotor copper heat**. The stator just supplies a steady DC field; it does not receive any return energy.

---

## 2.3 Method 3: REGENERATIVE BRAKING

### Operating Principle

Regenerative braking occurs when the rotor speed **exceeds the synchronous speed** in the **same direction** as the rotating magnetic field. The slip becomes **negative**:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} < 0 \quad \text{when} \quad N_r > N_s$$

Under this condition:
- The motor operates as an **induction generator**.
- The rotor current reverses direction (the slip term $R_2'/s$ becomes negative).
- The electromagnetic torque **reverses sign** → becomes a braking torque.
- Power flows **from the rotor → air gap → stator → back to the supply** (negative power flow).

### Mathematical Condition

The motoring torque equation is valid for negative $s$ as well. When $s < 0$:

$$T_{regen} = -\frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/\vert s\vert)}{\left(R_1 - R_2'/\vert s\vert\right)^2 + \left(X_1 + X_2'\right)^2}$$

The torque is **negative** (opposing rotation), confirming braking action.

### Why the Motor Cannot Self-Accelerate Infinitely

Even though $N_r > N_s$ gives braking, the very braking torque limits further acceleration. Equilibrium is reached when braking torque equals the driving torque of the load. In a loaded descending hoist, the load tries to accelerate the motor above $N_s$; the motor pushes back, returning energy to the grid, and the system stabilises at a speed slightly above $N_s$.

### Methods to Achieve Negative Slip

There are **two common ways** to force $N_r > N_s$:

1. **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Controlled Deceleration:** The VFD **reduces the supply frequency**, which in turn **reduces $N_s$**. If the rotor (and load inertia) was running at a higher speed, the rotor is now *above* the new, lower $N_s$. The motor becomes a generator, feeding energy back to the DC bus of the VFD.

2. **Naturally Overhauling Load:** A descending hoist or a downhill locomotive: the load itself tries to drag the rotor above $N_s$.

### Advantages

- **Energy is recovered** – not wasted as heat. This is the most energy-efficient braking method.
- No wear of components, no extra heat dissipation.
- Used in **regenerative VFDs**, electric vehicles (regenerative braking of trains, EVs, hybrid cars).
- Ideal for **high-inertia** or **frequently-braked** applications.

### Disadvantages

- Cannot bring the rotor to zero speed – it only works **above synchronous speed**.
- Requires a **bidirectional power converter** (active front-end rectifier) to pump energy back to the grid.
- More expensive than plugging or dynamic braking.

### Where Regenerative Braking is Used

- Electric locomotives (regenerative braking on descending grades).
- Hybrid and electric vehicles (Toyota Prius, Tesla, regenerative trains).
- Hoists and cranes (controlled lowering).
- Wind turbines (the turbine blades drive the induction generator above $N_s$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Recovery:** In a 10-ton electric locomotive descending a 5 km gradient, regenerative braking can recover several kWh per trip – a significant energy saving.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA REFERENCE – Memorise this table for KTU ESE 2024.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value / Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slip (General) | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | dimensionless | $0 < s < 1$ motoring |
| 2 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | rev/min | 1500, 1000, 750, 600 |
| 3 | Torque (general) | $T = \frac{3 V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$ | N·m | — |
| 4 | Plugging slip | $s_{plug} = 2 - s$ | dimensionless | $1 < s_{plug} < 2$ |
| 5 | Starting torque | $T_{st} = \frac{3 V_1^2 R_2'}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$ | N·m | $1.5$ to $2.5 \times T_{fl}$ |
| 6 | Max torque | $T_{max} = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s (R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2)^{1/2}}$ | N·m | at $s_{max} = R_2'/(X_1 + X_2')$ |
| 7 | Dynamic braking equiv. voltage | $V_{dc,eq} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} V_{dc}$ | volts | For star stator |
| 8 | Regen. braking slip | $s_{regen} < 0$ | dimensionless | $-1 < s < 0$ |
| 9 | Mechanical output | $P_m = T \cdot \omega_m = \frac{2\pi N_r T}{60}$ | watts | — |
| 10 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | watts | — |
| 11 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = T \cdot \omega_s$ | watts | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** The vertical bar `|` has been replaced by `\vert` in the formulas to keep the markdown table valid.

---

## 2.5 Comparison Matrix – The KTU "Must-Know" Table

| Parameter | Plugging | Dynamic Braking | Regenerative Braking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supply during braking** | 3-phase AC (reversed) | DC | 3-phase AC (normal) |
| **Final speed** | 0 (must disconnect) | 0 (natural stop) | Cannot reach 0 |
| **Slip region** | $1 < s < 2$ | Variable | $-1 < s < 0$ |
| **Energy destination** | Rotor heat | Rotor heat | Returned to supply |
| **Braking torque magnitude** | Very high | Medium–High | Medium |
| **Reverse rotation risk** | YES – need zero-speed switch | None | None |
| **Capital cost** | Low (contactor pair) | Low–Medium (DC source) | High (4-quadrant drive) |
| **Energy efficiency** | Low (all wasted as heat) | Low (all wasted as heat) | High (energy recovered) |
| **Application** | Printing, rolling mill | Elevator, crane | Locomotive, EV, hoist |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Slip During Plugging Equals (2 - s)

**Given:**
- Original synchronous speed: $N_s$
- Original rotor speed: $N_r$ (in the same direction as the original field)
- Original slip: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$

**Step 1:** The original rotor speed can be expressed in terms of slip.

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

**Step 2:** When two stator leads are swapped, the rotating magnetic field reverses direction. The new synchronous speed vectorially becomes $-N_s$.

$$N_{s,new} = -N_s$$

**Step 3:** The rotor inertia keeps $N_r$ unchanged in magnitude and direction (at the instant of plugging).

$$N_{r,new} = +N_r$$

**Step 4:** Compute the new slip.

$$s_{plug} = \frac{N_{s,new} - N_{r,new}}{N_{s,new}} = \frac{-N_s - N_r}{-N_s}$$

**Step 5:** Factor and simplify.

$$s_{plug} = \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = \frac{N_s}{N_s} + \frac{N_r}{N_s} = 1 + (1 - s)$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form.

$$\boxed{\;s_{plug} = 2 - s\;}$$

This derivation confirms the famous KTU result. For a motor running at $s = 0.04$ (4% slip), $s_{plug} = 1.96$, very close to standstill in the reversed field – which is why the rotor draws huge current.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Braking Torque at the Instant of Plugging

**Given:** Per-phase equivalent circuit of induction motor referred to stator, with the slip $s$ replaced by $(2-s)$ for plugging.

The rotor branch impedance is:

$$Z_{2,plug} = \frac{R_2'}{2-s} + j X_2'$$

**Step 1:** Compute the rotor current magnitude.

$$I_{2,plug} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{2-s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 2:** The air-gap power is $P_{ag} = 3 I_{2,plug}^2 \cdot R_2'/(2-s)$.

$$P_{ag,plug} = \frac{3 V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/(2-s))}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/(2-s)\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 3:** The electromagnetic torque is air-gap power divided by synchronous mechanical speed.

$$T_{plug} = \frac{P_{ag,plug}}{\omega_s}$$

**Step 4:** Final expression.

$$\boxed{\;T_{plug} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/(2-s))}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/(2-s)\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}\;}$$

Because $2-s$ is small, the term $R_2'/(2-s)$ is large, hence the rotor current surge and the large braking torque.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent RMS Voltage for Dynamic Braking

**Given:** A Y-connected stator with DC voltage $V_{dc}$ applied between two terminals.

**Step 1:** The DC current flowing through two stator windings in series produces a stationary MMF in the air gap. The peak value of the MMF (per pole pair) is:

$$\mathcal{F}_{dc} = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{N_1 k_{w1}}{P} \cdot I_{dc}$$

**Step 2:** An equivalent balanced 3-phase AC system with RMS voltage $V_{ac,eq}$ produces a peak MMF:

$$\mathcal{F}_{ac} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{N_1 k_{w1}}{P} \cdot \frac{V_{ac,eq}}{Z_{phase}}$$

**Step 3:** For the air-gap MMF to be equal, the relationship reduces to the well-known empirical form:

$$V_{ac,eq} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot V_{dc}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical check – for $V_{dc} = 100\,\text{V}$:

$$V_{ac,eq} = \frac{1.414}{1.732} \times 100 \approx 81.6\,\text{V}$$

This $V_{ac,eq}$ is then used in the standard torque equation with the appropriate slip.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Operating Regions of an Induction Machine

| Region | Slip $s$ | Rotor speed $N_r$ | Torque direction | Machine action | Power flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standstill | $s = 1$ | 0 | None (or starting) | Starting | AC in → Rotor Cu |
| Motoring | $0 < s < 1$ | $0 < N_r < N_s$ | Forward | Motor | AC in → Mechanical out |
| Synchronous (ideal) | $s = 0$ | $N_s$ | 0 | Synchronous (no torque) | None |
| Regenerative braking | $s < 0$ | $N_r > N_s$ | Reverse (braking) | Induction generator | Mechanical in → AC out |
| Plugging | $1 < s < 2$ | $N_r < 0$ in new reference | Reverse (braking) | Reverse motor | AC in → Rotor Cu (heat) |
| Reverse motoring | $0 < s < 1$ (in reverse) | $-N_s < N_r < 0$ | Reverse | Reverse motor | AC in → Mechanical out |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Vignette:** A common 14-mark question asks: *“Explain with a torque-slip curve the different operating regions of a 3-phase induction motor and identify the regions corresponding to plugging and regenerative braking.”* Drawing the curve with all four quadrants correctly is the key to full marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Negative-Slip Torque (Regenerative Braking)

**Starting equation:**

$$T = \frac{3 V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$$

**Step 1:** For regenerative braking, $s < 0$. Let $s = -\vert s\vert$.

$$T_{regen} = \frac{3 V_1^2 (R_2'/(-\vert s\vert))}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 + R_2'/(-\vert s\vert))^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify numerator and denominator.

$$T_{regen} = \frac{-3 V_1^2 (R_2'/\vert s\vert)}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 - R_2'/\vert s\vert)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$$

**Step 3:** The negative sign confirms that the torque is **opposing the rotation** – which is the desired braking torque.

$$\boxed{\;T_{regen} = -\frac{3 V_1^2 (R_2'/\vert s\vert)}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 - R_2'/\vert s\vert)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}\;}$$

**Step 4:** The mechanical power developed (per-phase equivalent) is:

$$P_{mech} = (1 - s) P_{ag} = (1 - (-\vert s\vert)) P_{ag} = (1 + \vert s\vert) P_{ag} > 0$$

But because $s < 0$, the rotor copper loss is also negative in the conventional sign convention, indicating that **mechanical energy is being converted to electrical energy** and is flowing back to the supply.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical interpretation:** When the load pushes the rotor faster than the rotating field, the rotor "leads" the field. By Lenz's law, the induced currents now exert a force that tries to **drag the rotor back** to synchronous speed – this is the braking action.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style – 7 Mark Sub-Part)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. It is operating at rated load and rated voltage. Calculate:
> 1. The synchronous speed.
> 2. The rotor speed.
> 3. The slip immediately after the motor is plugged.
> 4. The ratio of rotor copper loss during plugging to that during motoring.

**Step 1:** Synchronous speed.

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\,\text{rev/min}$$

**Step 2:** Rotor speed at full load.

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 (1 - 0.04) = 1440\,\text{rev/min}$$

**Step 3:** Slip during plugging.

$$s_{plug} = 2 - s = 2 - 0.04 = 1.96$$

**Step 4:** Rotor copper loss ratio.

The rotor copper loss is $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{ag}$. At the moment of plugging (assuming $P_{ag}$ approximately constant due to constant voltage), the ratio is:

$$\frac{P_{rcl,plug}}{P_{rcl,motor}} = \frac{s_{plug}}{s} = \frac{1.96}{0.04} = 49$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** Rotor copper loss becomes **49 times** higher during plugging compared to motoring. This is why plugging is energy-wasteful and dangerous thermally.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *slip* of a 3-phase induction motor. What is the value of slip during **plugging** if the motor was originally running at a slip of $s$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Slip is the relative difference between the synchronous speed $N_s$ of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. For normal motoring, $0 < s < 1$.

**Slip during plugging (1.5 marks):** When two stator phases are interchanged, the rotating field reverses direction. The new slip is:

$$s_{plug} = 2 - s$$

For example, if $s = 0.04$, then $s_{plug} = 1.96$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** List any **three differences** between regenerative braking and dynamic braking of a 3-phase induction motor.

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark per difference):**

| # | Regenerative Braking | Dynamic Braking |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy is **returned to the supply** (motor acts as induction generator). | Energy is **dissipated as heat** in the rotor circuit. |
| 2 | Occurs when rotor speed $N_r$ **exceeds** synchronous speed $N_s$ ($s < 0$). | Achieved by **disconnecting AC and applying DC** to the stator. |
| 3 | Motor **cannot stop at zero speed** – braking ceases as $N_r \to N_s$. | Motor **stops at zero speed** naturally as $N_r \to 0$. |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **plugging** method of braking a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the expression for slip during plugging. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 5%. Calculate: **(i)** the rotor speed at full load, **(ii)** the rotor speed at which regenerative braking begins, and **(iii)** the rotor speed at the moment of plugging. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) – 7 Marks

**Working Principle (3 marks):** In plugging, the supply connections to any two stator terminals are interchanged using a pair of contactors (forward and reverse). This reverses the direction of the rotating magnetic field. Since the rotor is still rotating in the original direction, the relative velocity between the rotor and the field becomes the sum of their speeds. A large braking torque is developed that decelerates the rotor.

**Sketch (2 marks):** A circuit diagram showing the three-phase supply, forward contactor, reverse contactor, motor terminals, and a zero-speed switch.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$s_{plug} = \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 1 + \frac{N_r}{N_s} = 1 + (1 - s) = 2 - s$$

> The braking torque is given by the standard torque equation with slip $s$ replaced by $(2 - s)$:
>
> $$T_{b,plug} = \frac{3 V_1^2 (R_2'/(2-s))}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 + R_2'/(2-s))^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$$

A **zero-speed relay** must disconnect the supply when $N_r \to 0$, otherwise the motor will reverse and accelerate.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) – 7 Marks

**Step 1: Synchronous speed (1 mark).**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\,\text{rev/min}$$

**Step 2: Rotor speed at full load (2 marks).**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1000 (1 - 0.05) = 950\,\text{rev/min}$$

**Step 3: Rotor speed at the start of regenerative braking (2 marks).**

Regenerative braking begins when $N_r$ slightly exceeds $N_s$. So:

$$N_{r,regen} > N_s = 1000\,\text{rev/min}$$

Regenerative braking initiates at $N_r = 1000\,\text{rev/min}$ (slip $s = 0$) and becomes effective for $N_r > 1000\,\text{rev/min}$.

**Step 4: Rotor speed at the moment of plugging (2 marks).**

Just before plugging, the motor runs at $N_r = 950\,\text{rev/min}$. The slip during plugging is:

$$s_{plug} = 2 - 0.05 = 1.95$$

The rotor speed remains **950 rev/min** at the instant of plugging (inertia keeps it constant for that instant). However, in the **new field reference**, the rotor appears to be running in the opposite direction at:

$$N_{r,ref} = -950\,\text{rev/min}$$

The braking torque decelerates this until $N_r = 0$.

> **Answer Summary:**
> - (i) $N_r = 950\,\text{rev/min}$
> - (ii) $N_r = 1000\,\text{rev/min}$ (just above $N_s$)
> - (iii) $N_r = 950\,\text{rev/min}$ (in original direction, decelerating to zero)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the **dynamic (rheostatic) braking** of a 3-phase induction motor. How is the braking torque produced? Mention one industrial application. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With the help of a torque-slip curve, identify and explain the regions corresponding to **(i) motoring, (ii) plugging, and (iii) regenerative braking** of a 3-phase induction motor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) – 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Dynamic braking, also called rheostatic braking, is the method of braking in which the kinetic energy of the rotating rotor is dissipated as heat in the rotor circuit, by disconnecting the AC supply and injecting a DC current into the stator.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. The 3-phase AC supply is disconnected from the stator.
2. A DC voltage (typically obtained from a rectifier) is applied across any two stator terminals.
3. The DC current produces a stationary (non-rotating) magnetic field in the air gap.
4. The rotor, still spinning, cuts this stationary field.
5. By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced in the rotor bars, causing a rotor current.
6. This rotor current interacts with the stationary field to produce a **braking torque** (Lenz's law) that opposes the rotation.

**Braking Torque Equation (2 marks):**

$$T_{db} = \frac{3 V_{dc,eq}^2 (R_2'/s)}{\omega_s \left[(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2\right]}$$

where $V_{dc,eq} = (\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}) V_{dc}$ for a Y-connected stator.

**Industrial Application (1 mark):** Dynamic braking is used in **electric elevators** to bring the cage to a smooth, controlled stop without risk of reversal, and in **machine tools** to arrest the spindle quickly.

**Advantage (1 mark):** It cannot cause reverse rotation – the motor naturally stops at zero.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) – 7 Marks

**Torque-Slip Curve (3 marks for drawing):**

The student must draw the characteristic torque-slip curve with $s$ on the negative side of the $y$-axis, the four regions clearly marked:

```
   T |
     |           T_max
   + |---------*
     |        / \
   + |       /   \
   0 +------*-----+------*---------> s
     |     /|     |     /|
   - |    / |     |    / |  Tmin (negative)
     |   /  |     |   /
   - |  /   |     |  /
     | /    |     | /
     |/     |     |/
     +------+-----+---------
     0     s_max  1     2
            |     |     |
        Motoring Plug Regen
```

**Region identification (4 marks – 1 for each identification + 1 for explanation):**

- **Motoring (1.5 marks):** $0 < s < 1$. Torque is positive, rotor rotates in the same direction as the field, mechanical power is delivered to the load.
- **Plugging (1.5 marks):** $1 < s < 2$. The slip is greater than unity because the field is reversed; torque is negative, producing braking action. The rotor is being decelerated from its original forward speed to zero.
- **Regenerative Braking (1 mark):** $s < 0$. The rotor speed exceeds synchronous speed. The torque is negative, and the machine acts as an induction generator, returning energy to the supply. Most commonly observed in overhauling loads (descending hoist) or with VFD-controlled deceleration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU ESE 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Plugging slip:** Writing $2s$ instead of $2 - s$. The former gives $0.08$ for $s = 0.04$ (wrong), the correct is $1.96$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing dynamic braking with regenerative:** Many students state that during dynamic braking "energy is returned to the supply" – this is **wrong**. Energy is **dissipated as heat in the rotor**. **Lose 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the zero-speed switch in plugging:** Examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for the safety switch. Omit it and lose that mark.
> 4. **Not drawing the four quadrants of the torque-slip curve:** A 7-mark question on operating regions almost always requires the full curve with both positive and negative torque/slip axes. Drawing only the motoring quadrant loses **3 marks**.
> 5. **Writing $V_{dc,eq} = V_{dc}$ for dynamic braking:** The correct relation is $V_{ac,eq} = (\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}) V_{dc}$ for a Y-connected stator. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 6. **In regenerative braking, not mentioning the VFD or overhauling load mechanism:** Examiners expect an explicit statement of *how* $N_r$ exceeds $N_s$. Simply saying "slip is negative" without justification is incomplete.
> 7. **Unit errors:** Writing slip in percent without clarifying (slip is dimensionless but often expressed as a percentage like $s = 4\%$). Be consistent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and revise the night before your KTU ESE 2024 exam.**

### Braking of Induction Motors – At a Glance

- **Why brake?** Fast stop, overspeed control, load holding, controlled deceleration.
- **Three methods:** Plugging, Dynamic, Regenerative.

### Plugging – Key Facts

- Swap any **two** of the three stator leads.
- Slip during plugging: $s_{plug} = 2 - s$.
- Operates in slip region $1 < s < 2$.
- Very high current surge (5–7× normal).
- **Mandatory** zero-speed switch to prevent reverse rotation.
- Energy dissipated as rotor copper heat.
- Rotor copper loss during plugging is $(2-s)/s$ times that during motoring.
- Used in: Printing presses, rolling mills, textile machinery.

### Dynamic Braking – Key Facts

- Disconnect AC, inject DC into stator.
- Creates a **stationary** magnetic field.
- Rotor cuts field → induced rotor current → braking torque.
- Equivalent AC voltage: $V_{ac,eq} = (\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}) V_{dc}$ (Y-connected).
- Braking torque vanishes as $N_r \to 0$ – natural stop, no reverse rotation.
- Energy dissipated in rotor resistance.
- Used in: Elevators, cranes, machine tools, conveyor belts.

### Regenerative Braking – Key Facts

- Occurs when $N_r > N_s$ → slip $s < 0$.
- Machine operates as an **induction generator**.
- Energy is **returned to the supply** (energy-efficient).
- Cannot stop at zero speed (only above $N_s$).
- Achieved by VFD frequency reduction or overhauling loads.
- Used in: Electric locomotives, electric vehicles, hybrid cars, wind turbines, hoists with overhauling loads.

### Formulae to Memorise

- Slip: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$
- Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P$
- Plugging slip: $s_{plug} = 2 - s$
- Torque equation: $T = (3 V_1^2 (R_2'/s)) / (\omega_s [(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2])$
- Dynamic brake equivalent voltage: $V_{ac,eq} = (\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}) V_{dc}$
- Air-gap power: $P_{ag} = T \cdot \omega_s$
- Rotor copper loss: $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{ag}$

### KTU Exam Day Strategy

1. Always draw the circuit diagram first (3-phase supply + contactors + motor + control switch).
2. Always annotate the **slip value** in the operating region.
3. Always mention the **safety interlock** (zero-speed switch for plugging).
4. Always write the **energy destination** (heat vs. grid).
5. In 14-mark questions, dedicate 1 mark's worth of content to a **real-world example**.

topics.title: Speed Control - Stator Voltage Control - V/f Control - Rotor Resistance Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET402 - SYNCHRONOUS & INDUCTION MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speed Control - Stator Voltage Control - V/f Control - Rotor Resistance Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Speed Control of Three-Phase Induction Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed Control of Induction Motor:** The process of deliberately varying the operating speed of a three-phase induction motor below or above its base speed by adjusting electrical parameters (stator voltage, supply frequency, rotor resistance, pole count, or slip) to match the mechanical load requirements, while maintaining adequate torque, efficiency, and stability characteristics.

The synchronous speed of an induction motor is given by:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times f}{P}$$

where:
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz**
- $P$ = Number of poles

The actual rotor speed is:

$$N_r = N_s(1 - s)$$

where $s$ = slip of the motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes three primary methods: **Stator Voltage Control**, **V/f Control**, and **Rotor Resistance Control** — each suited to different load and application profiles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Gear" Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle with a fixed gear ratio:
- **Stator Voltage Control** = pressing the brake slightly. You reduce the "push" to the wheel, so the bike slows down. But you also lose leg power.
- **V/f Control** = changing both the pedaling speed AND the gear simultaneously. You maintain the same leg effort but travel at a different speed — this is the most efficient method.
- **Rotor Resistance Control** = adding friction to the wheel hub. The wheel doesn't spin as fast, but the motor generates more heat — useful only for short bursts.

### 1.3 Why Speed Control is Essential in Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Induction motors are the **workhorses of industry**, consuming approximately **65-70%** of all electrical energy used in industrial applications globally. Speed control directly impacts process quality, energy savings, and mechanical design flexibility.

Applications include:
- Conveyor belts (variable speeds for different products)
- Pumps and fans (energy savings at part-load)
- Electric vehicles (traction control)
- Machine tools (spindle speed variation)
- HVAC systems (air volume control)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. Fundamental Principles of Speed Control

The electromagnetic torque developed by a three-phase induction motor is given by the **Kloss equation**:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

The maximum (breakdown) torque is:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2X_2}$$

The slip at maximum torque:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These three equations form the **analytical backbone** of every speed control method. Students must memorize them — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.

## 3. Method 1: Stator Voltage Control

### 3.1 Principle of Operation

In this method, the **stator supply voltage** is varied while keeping the **supply frequency constant**. Since torque is proportional to the square of the voltage ($T \propto V_1^2$), reducing voltage reduces the torque capability.

### 3.2 How it Works — Step by Step

- The motor is supplied through a variable AC voltage source (typically a 3-phase **autotransformer** or **thyristor-based AC voltage controller**).
- When voltage $V_1$ is reduced, the air-gap flux $\Phi$ decreases (since $V_1 \approx 4.44 f N \Phi K_w$).
- Maximum torque $T_{max} \propto V_1^2$ drops significantly.
- The operating point shifts to a higher slip, meaning lower speed.
- The motor draws less current at light loads.

### 3.3 Torque-Speed Characteristics

| Parameter | Effect |
|-----------|--------|
| Stator Voltage ($V_1$) | Decreases |
| Maximum Torque ($T_{max}$) | Decreases as $V_1^2$ |
| Slip at $T_{max}$ ($s_m$) | Remains **unchanged** |
| Starting Torque | Decreases as $V_1^2$ |
| No-load speed | Nearly unchanged |

### 3.4 Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> Stator voltage control is **inefficient and produces significant heat** at low speeds. The rotor must dissipate the increased slip energy ($s \cdot P_{ag}$) as heat. It is suitable only for **fan and pump-type loads** where load torque $\propto N^2$.

## 4. Method 2: V/f Control (Variable Voltage Variable Frequency — VVVF)

### 4.1 Principle of Operation

This is the **most widely used and efficient** method of speed control. Both **voltage AND frequency are varied simultaneously** to maintain a constant **air-gap flux**.

### 4.2 The "Why" — Constant Flux Condition

The EMF equation of the stator is:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w$$

Rearranging for flux:

$$\Phi = \frac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w}$$

If we want flux $\Phi$ to remain **constant**, we must keep the ratio $V_1/f$ (or $E_1/f$) **constant**.

### 4.3 Operational Regions

**Region 1: Below Base Speed (Constant Torque Region)**
- Both $V_1$ and $f$ are reduced proportionally.
- $V_1/f$ = constant → flux $\Phi$ = constant.
- Maximum torque remains **constant**.
- Used for constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists).

**Region 2: Above Base Speed (Constant Power Region)**
- Frequency is increased above rated value.
- Voltage cannot exceed rated value (insulation limit).
- $V_1/f$ decreases → flux decreases.
- Maximum torque decreases as $1/f$.
- Power remains approximately constant.
- Used for spindle drives, traction applications.

**Region 3: Low-Speed Boost**
- To compensate for stator voltage drop ($I_1 R_1$), a small voltage boost is added at very low frequencies.
- Maintains rated torque at low speeds.

### 4.4 Torque-Speed Characteristics

| Region | $f$ | $V_1$ | $V_1/f$ | Flux $\Phi$ | $T_{max}$ | Power |
|--------|-----|-------|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| Below base | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Constant | Constant | Constant | Decreases |
| Above base | $\uparrow$ | Constant (max) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The V/f method maintains **constant maximum torque** over a wide speed range below base speed — this is why it dominates industrial drives.

## 5. Method 3: Rotor Resistance Control

### 5.1 Principle of Operation

This method is applicable **only to slip-ring (wound rotor) induction motors**. External resistance is added to the rotor circuit through slip rings, which alters the rotor's effective resistance.

### 5.2 How it Works

From the torque equation, slip at maximum torque:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{X_2}$$

By adding external resistance $R_{ext}$:
- $s_m$ increases → maximum torque point shifts to **higher slip values**.
- $T_{max}$ remains **unchanged** (since $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$).
- The torque-speed curve becomes "softer" and more linear.
- Starting torque **increases** dramatically.

### 5.3 Speed Control Range

For a given load torque $T_L$, the operating slip changes from $s_1$ (without external resistance) to $s_2$ (with external resistance):

$$s_2 = s_1 \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{R_2}$$

### 5.4 Advantages and Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|---------------|
| High starting torque | Limited to slip-ring motors |
| Smooth speed control | Large energy loss in external resistance |
| Simple implementation | Low efficiency at low speeds |
| Better starting current | Requires frequent maintenance of slip rings |

### 5.5 Applications

- **Cranes and hoists** (high starting torque, intermittent operation)
- **Elevators** (smooth acceleration)
- **Washing machines** (variable agitation speeds)
- **Mine winders**

## 6. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---------|------------|-------------|------|
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = 120f/P$ | Rotating magnetic field speed | rpm |
| Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ | Actual shaft speed | rpm |
| Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Relative speed difference | dimensionless |
| EMF Equation | $E_1 = 4.44 f N_{ph} \Phi K_w$ | Stator induced EMF | V |
| Air-gap Flux | $\Phi = V_1 / (4.44 f N_{ph} K_w)$ | Working flux | Wb |
| Max Torque | $T_{max} = 3V_1^2 / (4\pi N_s X_2)$ | Breakdown torque | Nm |
| Slip at Max Torque | $s_m = R_2 / X_2$ | Critical slip | dimensionless |
| Full-load Torque | $T_{fl} = P_{out} / \omega_r$ | Shaft torque | Nm |
| Slip with $R_{ext}$ | $s_2 = s_1(R_2 + R_{ext})/R_2$ | New operating slip | dimensionless |
| Slip Power | $P_{slip} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | Power dissipated as rotor Cu loss | W |
| Rotor Cu Loss | $P_{cu2} = 3 I_2^2 R_2$ | Heat in rotor | W |
| Mechanical Power | $P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$ | Developed power | W |
| Air-gap Power | $P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | Power crossing air gap | W |
| Constant V/f Torque | $T = 3/2 \cdot (V/f)^2 \cdot (f R_2/s) / (2\pi \cdot [(R_2/s)^2 + (2\pi f L_2)^2])$ | Torque under V/f control | Nm |
| Power Rating | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | Shaft power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes used in the table** — absolute values are represented using context or parentheses to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 7. Derivation 1: Slip at Maximum Torque

Starting from the **torque equation**:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

To find maximum torque, we differentiate $T_e$ with respect to slip $s$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$$

Let us substitute $R_2/s = R$ for simplicity. Then:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{R}{R^2 + X_2^2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $R$ (and noting that $R = R_2/s$ implies $dR = -R_2/s^2 \, ds$):

$$\frac{d}{dR}\left[\frac{R}{R^2 + X_2^2}\right] = \frac{(R^2 + X_2^2) - R(2R)}{(R^2 + X_2^2)^2} = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$R^2 + X_2^2 - 2R^2 = 0$$

$$X_2^2 - R^2 = 0$$

$$R = X_2$$

Substituting back $R = R_2/s$:

$$\frac{R_2}{s_m} = X_2$$

$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

**Interpretation:** The slip at which maximum torque occurs depends **only on rotor parameters** ($R_2$ and $X_2$) and is **independent of stator voltage**.

## 8. Derivation 2: Maximum Torque Magnitude

Substituting $s = s_m = R_2/X_2$ back into the torque equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot X_2}{X_2^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot X_2}{2X_2^2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2X_2} = \frac{3V_1^2}{4\pi N_s X_2}}$$

**Key Conclusions:**

$$\frac{T_{max,\,new}}{T_{max,\,rated}} = \left(\frac{V_{1,\,new}}{V_{1,\,rated}}\right)^2$$

$$s_{m,\,new} = s_{m,\,rated} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{R_2}$$

## 9. Derivation 3: Constant V/f Principle

We start from the EMF equation of the stator:

$$V_1 \approx E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi_m \, K_w$$

For **constant flux** $\Phi_m = $ constant, we require:

$$\frac{V_1}{f} = 4.44 \, N_{ph} \, \Phi_m \, K_w = \text{constant}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_1}{f} = \text{constant}}$$

**Verification through torque equation under V/f control:**

Substituting $V_1 = k \cdot f$ (where $k$ is a constant) into the torque expression with $X_2 = 2\pi f L_2$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{(kf)^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + (2\pi f L_2)^2}$$

Since $N_s = 120f/P$ and $(kf)^2$ cancels appropriately with $(2\pi f L_2)^2$ at all frequencies:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot k^2}{4\pi \cdot (120/P) \cdot 2\pi L_2} = \text{constant}$$

**This proves that maximum torque remains constant** for all frequencies below base frequency, validating the constant-torque region of V/f control.

## 10. Derivation 4: Speed Change with Rotor Resistance

Consider a load torque $T_L$ at original slip $s_1$ with rotor resistance $R_2$:

$$T_L = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{R_2/s_1}{(R_2/s_1)^2 + X_2^2}$$

After adding $R_{ext}$, the new slip is $s_2$. For the same load torque in the operating region (small slip approximation where $R_2/s \gg X_2$):

$$T_L \approx \frac{3 V_1^2}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{s}{R_2}$$

Setting torques equal (assuming operation in linear region):

$$\frac{s_1}{R_2} = \frac{s_2}{R_2 + R_{ext}}$$

$$\boxed{s_2 = s_1 \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{R_2}}$$

The new speed:

$$N_{r2} = N_s(1 - s_2) = N_s\left(1 - s_1 \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{R_2}\right)$$

## 11. Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has full-load slip of 4%. If the supply voltage is reduced to 70% of rated value, find the new operating slip assuming the load torque is proportional to the square of speed (fan-type load).

**Given:**
- $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$, $s_{fl} = 0.04$
- $V_1^{new} = 0.7 V_{rated}$

**Solution:**

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

Original rotor speed:

$$N_{r1} = 1500(1 - 0.04) = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$

For fan-type load: $T_L \propto N^2 \propto (1-s)^2$

In the linear operating region ($s$ small), torque $\propto V_1^2 \cdot s/R_2$:

$$\frac{T_{L,\,new}}{T_{L,\,old}} = \left(\frac{V_{1,\,new}}{V_{1,\,old}}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{s_{new}}{s_{old}}$$

Setting this equal to the load torque ratio:

$$\frac{(1 - s_{new})^2}{(1 - s_{old})^2} = (0.7)^2 \cdot \frac{s_{new}}{s_{old}}$$

Substituting $s_{old} = 0.04$:

$$\frac{(1 - s_{new})^2}{0.9216} = 0.49 \cdot \frac{s_{new}}{0.04}$$

$$(1 - s_{new})^2 = 0.49 \cdot 0.9216 \cdot \frac{s_{new}}{0.04} = 11.29 \cdot s_{new}$$

Expanding:

$$1 - 2s_{new} + s_{new}^2 = 11.29 \, s_{new}$$

$$s_{new}^2 - 13.29 \, s_{new} + 1 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$s_{new} = \frac{13.29 \pm \sqrt{176.6 - 4}}{2} = \frac{13.29 \pm \sqrt{172.6}}{2}$$

$$s_{new} = \frac{13.29 \pm 13.14}{2}$$

Taking the physically meaningful root (less than 1):

$$\boxed{s_{new} = 0.075 \text{ or } 7.5\%}$$

**Verification:** New rotor speed = $1500(1 - 0.075) = 1387.5$ rpm — a 52.5 rpm reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 12. Block Diagram: V/f Control System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 50/60 Hz] --> B[Converter Rectifier]
    B --> C[DC Link Capacitor Filter]
    C --> D[Inverter PWM Control]
    E[Reference Frequency f ref] --> F[Control Logic Unit]
    G[Reference Voltage V ref] --> F
    F --> H[V or f Generator]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Variable V and Variable f Output]
    I --> J[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    J --> K[Speed Sensor Encoder]
    K --> L[Feedback Signal]

    J --> M[Mechanical Load]
```

## 13. Comparison Block Diagram: Three Speed Control Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Induction Motor] --> B{Speed Control Method}
    B -->|Method 1| C[Stator Voltage Control]
    B -->|Method 2| D[V or f Control]
    B -->|Method 3| E[Rotor Resistance Control]
    C --> F[Autotransformer OR Thyristor AC Controller]
    D --> G[Inverter VVVF Drive]
    E --> H[External Rotor Resistance via Slip Rings]

    G --> A
    H --> A
```

## 14. Torque-Speed Characteristics — Qualitative Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Synchronous Speed Ns] --> B[Operating Region]
    B --> C[Low Slip Region s 0.02 to 0.05]
    B --> D[High Slip Region s 0.05 to 0.5]
    C --> E[Stator Voltage Control Effective]
    C --> F[V or f Control Highly Effective]
    D --> G[Rotor Resistance Control Effective]
    E --> H[Reduced Torque Capability T max drops as V squared]
    F --> I[Constant T max Constant Power Region]
    G --> J[Slip Increases T max Same Magnitude Different Location]
```

## 15. Power Flow Diagram in Induction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Input P in] --> B[Stator Copper Loss 3 I1 squared R1]
    A --> C[Core Loss P core]
    B --> D[Air gap Power P ag]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Rotor Copper Loss s times P ag]
    D --> F[Mechanical Power Developed P m]
    E --> G[Rotational Losses Friction and Windage]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Shaft Output Power P out]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 16. Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define V/f Control. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

V/f (Voltage-to-Frequency) control, also known as VVVF (Variable Voltage Variable Frequency) control, is a method of speed control for three-phase induction motors in which **both the stator supply voltage and frequency are varied simultaneously** to maintain a constant air-gap flux.

The fundamental relationship is:

$$\frac{V_1}{f} = \text{constant} = 4.44 \, N_{ph} \, \Phi_m \, K_w$$

This ensures that:
1. Air-gap flux $\Phi_m$ remains constant at its rated value.
2. Maximum (breakdown) torque remains constant.
3. The motor delivers rated torque at all speeds below base speed.
4. It provides a wide speed range with high efficiency and good transient response.

V/f control is implemented using power electronic converters (rectifier-inverter combinations) and is the standard method used in modern industrial drives, including variable frequency drives (VFDs) and electric vehicle traction systems.

### Question 2: Why is rotor resistance control not used for continuous-duty industrial applications? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Rotor resistance control is unsuitable for continuous-duty industrial applications due to the following reasons:

1. **High Power Loss:** The external resistance dissipates a large amount of power as heat ($P_{loss} = 3 I_2^2 R_{ext}$), reducing overall efficiency significantly at low speeds.

2. **Slip Energy Dissipation:** The slip power ($s \cdot P_{ag}$) is wasted as heat in the rotor circuit resistors, which is uneconomical for continuous operation.

3. **Cooling Requirements:** The heat generated requires bulky cooling arrangements, increasing the size and cost of the system.

4. **Limited Motor Type:** Applicable only to **slip-ring (wound rotor) motors**, which are more expensive and require more maintenance than squirrel-cage motors.

5. **Speed Variation with Load:** The speed is heavily dependent on load, leading to poor speed regulation in continuous-duty cycles.

Therefore, this method is restricted to **intermittent-duty applications** like cranes, hoists, and elevators where high starting torque is essential.

---

## 17. Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of suitable equations, explain the V/f method of speed control of a three-phase induction motor. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
V/f control is the most popular method of speed control for three-phase induction motors. It enables smooth, efficient speed control both below and above base (synchronous) speed.

**Working Principle:**
The synchronous speed of an induction motor is:

$$N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$$

Therefore, speed can be controlled by varying the supply frequency $f$. However, varying $f$ alone changes the air-gap flux:

$$\Phi = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w}$$

To keep flux constant, voltage must be varied proportionally with frequency, i.e., $V_1/f = $ constant.

**Operating Regions:**

**Region 1: Below Base Speed ($f < f_{rated}$)**
- $V_1$ and $f$ are reduced in the same ratio.
- Flux and maximum torque remain constant.
- Constant torque region suitable for conveyors, hoists.

**Region 2: Above Base Speed ($f > f_{rated}$)**
- Voltage is held at rated value (insulation limit).
- Flux reduces as $1/f$.
- Power remains approximately constant (field weakening region).

**Torque Equations:**
For constant $V_1/f = k$ and $X_2 = 2\pi f L_2$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 k^2}{4\pi N_s \cdot 2\pi L_2} = \text{constant (below base speed)}$$

**Advantages:**
1. Wide speed range (typically 20:1 or better)
2. Constant torque capability below base speed
3. High efficiency across the speed range
4. Smooth acceleration and deceleration
5. Good transient response

**Limitations:**
1. Requires complex power electronic converter (inverter)
2. Cost is higher than simple voltage control
3. Voltage drop across stator resistance at low frequencies reduces torque
4. Above base speed, torque reduces (field weakening)

---

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has rotor resistance and standstill reactance of 0.1 Ω and 0.5 Ω per phase respectively. Calculate: **(i)** the slip at maximum torque, and **(ii)** the maximum torque, if the supply voltage is 400 V. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $R_2 = 0.1$ Ω, $X_2 = 0.5$ Ω
- $V_{L} = 400$ V (Line voltage)

**Step 1: Calculate Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Phase Voltage**

$$V_1 = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Slip at Maximum Torque**

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2 \text{ (or 20%)}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Torque**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_1^2}{4\pi N_s X_2} \text{ (with N_s in rps)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2X_2}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{2 \times 104.72 \times 2 \times 0.5}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 53,333.7}{209.44}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{160,001}{209.44} = 763.96 \text{ Nm}$$

**Results:**
$$\boxed{s_m = 0.2 \text{ (20%)} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{max} \approx 764 \text{ Nm}}$$

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the method of rotor resistance speed control of slip-ring induction motor. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Constructional Setup:**
A slip-ring (wound rotor) induction motor has three slip rings mounted on the shaft, connected to the rotor winding. External resistors can be connected to the rotor circuit through brush gear, allowing variation of effective rotor resistance.

**Working Principle:**
From the torque equation, the slip at maximum torque is:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{X_2}$$

By varying $R_{ext}$:
- $s_m$ shifts to higher values
- $T_{max}$ remains **constant** (independent of rotor resistance)
- Operating point moves to higher slip (lower speed) for the same load torque

**Derivation of Speed Relation:**
For a constant load torque in the low-slip operating region:

$$T_L \approx \frac{3 V_1^2}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{s}{R_2 + R_{ext}}$$

For the same load torque at two different resistances:

$$\frac{s_1}{R_2} = \frac{s_2}{R_2 + R_{ext}}$$

$$s_2 = s_1 \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{R_2}$$

**Diagram (Circuit):**
Stator → 3-phase AC supply
Rotor → Y-connected winding → Slip rings → Brushes → External star-connected resistance bank with tapping points for stepped control or continuously variable using liquid rheostat.

**Torque-Speed Curves:**
A family of torque-speed curves is obtained as $R_{ext}$ increases:
- All curves have the **same $T_{max}$**
- $s_m$ increases progressively
- Starting torque increases significantly (important for heavy-load starting)

**Speed Regulation:**
- Below rated torque: good speed variation
- Above rated torque: limited variation
- Speed decreases as $R_{ext}$ increases (for same load)

**Practical Considerations:**
- External resistance must be rated for full-load rotor current
- Used in conjunction with a controller for smooth operation
- Energy wasted as heat in $R_{ext}$

---

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor develops a torque of 100 Nm at a slip of 4% with the rotor resistance of 0.5 Ω per phase. If the load torque remains constant, determine the additional rotor resistance required to develop the same torque at a slip of 12%. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$
- $T_1 = 100$ Nm at $s_1 = 0.04$, $R_2 = 0.5$ Ω

**Step 1: Calculate Rotor Reactance from Initial Condition**

In the low-slip region, torque approximation:

$$T_1 = \frac{3 V_1^2 \cdot s_1}{2\pi N_s \cdot R_2}$$

Solving for $X_2$ requires the exact formula. Let's use the exact torque equation:

$$T_1 = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{R_2/s_1}{(R_2/s_1)^2 + X_2^2}$$

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 25 \text{ rps}$$

$$\omega_s = 2\pi \times 25 = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$R_2/s_1 = \frac{0.5}{0.04} = 12.5 \text{ Ω}$$

Substituting:

$$100 = \frac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{2\pi \times 25} \cdot \frac{12.5}{12.5^2 + X_2^2}$$

$$100 = \frac{160,000}{157.08} \cdot \frac{12.5}{156.25 + X_2^2}$$

$$100 = 1018.6 \cdot \frac{12.5}{156.25 + X_2^2}$$

$$\frac{100 \times (156.25 + X_2^2)}{12.5} = 1018.6$$

$$156.25 + X_2^2 = 127.32$$

$$X_2^2 = -28.93 \text{ (negative - check calculation)}$$

**Correction using more accurate method:**

The approximation $s \cdot X_2 \ll R_2$ may not hold. Let us use the slip ratio method directly.

**Step 2: Direct Slip Proportionality Method**

For constant torque operation in the maximum torque region, we can use:

$$\frac{s_1}{s_{m1}} = \frac{s_2}{s_{m2}}$$

when operating on the stable side of the torque-slip curve, where $s_m = R_2/X_2$.

But the **simplest and most KTU-accepted approach** for this problem type:

For two different slips delivering the same torque:

$$\frac{R_2/s_1}{(R_2/s_1)^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{(R_2+R_{ext})/s_2}{[(R_2+R_{ext})/s_2]^2 + X_2^2}$$

Given the small slip, we approximate $R_2/s \gg X_2$:

$$T \approx \frac{3V_1^2 s}{2\pi N_s R_2}$$

For same torque: $s_1/R_2 = s_2/(R_2 + R_{ext})$:

$$R_2 + R_{ext} = R_2 \cdot \frac{s_2}{s_1} = 0.5 \times \frac{0.12}{0.04} = 1.5 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_{ext} = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ Ω}$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{R_{ext} = 1.0 \text{ Ω per phase}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** New speed = $1500(1 - 0.12) = 1320$ rpm. Old speed = $1500(1 - 0.04) = 1440$ rpm. Speed reduction = 120 rpm, achieved through added rotor resistance.

---

## 18. KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N_s$ from rpm to rps** in the torque formula $T = P/\omega$. Use $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$ — losing 1-2 marks here is very common.
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_L$ (line voltage) with $V_{ph}$ (phase voltage)**. Always use $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected motors and $V_{ph} = V_L$ for delta-connected motors.
>
> 3. **In V/f control questions**, students often forget the **low-speed voltage boost** to compensate for stator resistance drop, losing the "constant torque" property.
>
> 4. **Stator Voltage Control** is suitable only for **fan/pump-type loads** — stating it as a "general-purpose method" is a common wrong answer.
>
> 5. **Rotor resistance control** is restricted to **slip-ring motors only** — always mention this in your answer.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing torque in N-mm instead of N-m) — KTU examiners deduct at least 0.5 mark.
>
> 7. **Not drawing the torque-speed characteristics** when the question asks for an explanation — a labeled diagram is mandatory and fetches at least 1-2 marks.

---

## 19. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Examinations:**

### Key Conceptual Points

- The **synchronous speed** of an induction motor is given by $N_s = 120f/P$ and depends only on supply frequency and pole count.
- The **rotor speed** is always less than synchronous speed by the slip factor: $N_r = N_s(1-s)$.
- The **electromagnetic torque** is proportional to the square of supply voltage: $T \propto V_1^2$.
- The **maximum torque** is independent of rotor resistance: $T_{max} = 3V_1^2 / (4\pi N_s X_2)$.
- The **slip at maximum torque** is directly proportional to rotor resistance: $s_m = R_2/X_2$.

### Method 1: Stator Voltage Control — Quick Facts

- Vary $V_1$, keep $f$ constant.
- $T_{max}$ decreases as $V_1^2$.
- $s_m$ unchanged.
- Suitable for **fan and pump loads** only.
- Implemented using **autotransformer** or **thyristor AC voltage controller**.

### Method 2: V/f Control — Quick Facts

- Vary both $V_1$ and $f$ such that $V_1/f$ = constant.
- Air-gap flux $\Phi$ remains constant.
- Below base speed: **constant torque region** ($T_{max}$ unchanged).
- Above base speed: **constant power region** (field weakening).
- Implemented using **rectifier-inverter VFD topology**.
- Industry-standard method, widest application range.

### Method 3: Rotor Resistance Control — Quick Facts

- Vary $R_{ext}$ in rotor circuit (slip-ring motors only).
- $T_{max}$ unchanged; $s_m$ increases.
- High starting torque capability.
- Large slip power wasted as heat — **low efficiency at low speeds**.
- Used in **cranes, hoists, elevators** (intermittent duty).

### Critical Numerical Tips

- Always express $N_s$ in **rps (revolutions per second)** for torque formulas: $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$.
- For $V_L = 400$ V star-connected: $V_{ph} = 230.94$ V.
- For $V_L = 400$ V delta-connected: $V_{ph} = 400$ V.
- Slip $s$ is **dimensionless** — convert percentage to decimal: 4% → 0.04.
- In the **low-slip region** (s < $s_m$), torque is approximately linear with slip: $T \approx K \cdot s$.
- In the **high-slip region** (s > $s_m$), torque is approximately inversely proportional to slip: $T \approx K/s$.

### Formula Memory Aids

- **EMF equation** is the foundation: $E = 4.44 f N \Phi K_w$ — flux is inversely proportional to frequency.
- **Kloss equation** is the master torque formula — used in 80% of Part B questions.
- **For constant torque V/f region**: $T_{max}$ is independent of $f$ (only $V_1/f$ ratio matters).

### Common KTU Question Patterns

1. **Derive $s_m = R_2/X_2$** — frequently asked (3 or 7 marks).
2. **Show that $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$** — classic derivation.
3. **Calculate new slip/rpm with changed voltage or resistance** — numerical heavy.
4. **Compare the three methods** in a tabular form — frequently in Part A.
5. **Explain V/f control with block diagram** — almost every KTU paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **torque-speed characteristics** (with proper labels: $T_{max}$, $s_m$, operating point, and the three family of curves for different $V_1$ or $R_{ext}$). This single diagram can fetch you **2-3 bonus marks** in Part B answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
