## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain briefly using the double revolving field theory. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A single-phase induction motor is not self-starting because the alternating flux produced by a single-phase stator winding is purely **pulsating** along a fixed axis and does not rotate in space.

According to the **Double Revolving Field Theory**, this pulsating flux of magnitude $\phi_m\cos(\omega t)$ can be mathematically resolved into two equal-amplitude rotating flux vectors, each of magnitude $\phi_m/2$, rotating in **opposite directions** at synchronous speed $N_s$.

At standstill ($N_r = 0$, $s = 1$), the forward slip is $s_f = 1$ and the backward slip is $s_b = 2 - 1 = 1$. Since the rotor reacts **identically** to both fields (equal slip), the forward torque equals the backward torque, producing a **net starting torque of zero**. Hence, the motor cannot start on its own. Once the rotor is manually spun in either direction, the slips become unequal, and the motor develops a net torque in the direction of spin.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Compare split-phase and capacitor-start single-phase induction motors based on (i) starting torque, (ii) phase split angle, and (iii) one typical application. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Split-Phase Motor | Capacitor-Start Motor |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Starting Torque** | Low (1.5× to 2× Full Load Torque) | High (3× to 4.5× Full Load Torque) |
| **Phase Split Angle** | 25° to 35° (poor) | 70° to 90° (excellent) |
| **Starting Element** | No capacitor; high-resistance auxiliary winding | Capacitor in series with auxiliary winding (disconnected by centrifugal switch) |
| **Typical Application** | Washing machines, small fans, oil burners | Compressors, pumps, conveyors, refrigerators |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1**

**(a) Describe the construction and working of a split-phase induction motor with a neat sketch. Discuss its torque-speed characteristics. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A split-phase motor consists of:
1. **Stator:** A laminated silicon steel core with two windings placed **90° apart** in space:
   - **Main (Running) Winding:** Low resistance, high reactance (thick wire, more turns).
   - **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding:** High resistance, low reactance (thin wire, fewer turns).
2. **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type, identical to three-phase induction motor rotor.
3. **Centrifugal Switch:** Mounted on the rotor shaft, keeps the auxiliary winding connected at start and **disconnects it at 70%–80% of synchronous speed**.

**Working Principle:**
- When the motor is switched ON, both windings are energized simultaneously.
- Because the auxiliary winding has a higher resistance-to-reactance ratio, the current $I_a$ in it lags the voltage by a **smaller angle** than $I_m$ in the main winding.
- This creates a **phase difference of 25° to 35°** between the two currents.
- Combined with the **90° spatial displacement** of the windings, a weak **rotating magnetic field** is established, which produces a starting torque of about **1.5× to 2× the full-load torque**.
- Once the rotor reaches 75%–80% of $N_s$, the centrifugal switch opens and disconnects the auxiliary winding. The motor then continues to run on the main winding alone.

**Torque-Speed Characteristics:**
- Starting torque: 1.5×–2× FLT
- Pull-out torque: ~2.5×–3× FLT (at around 75% of $N_s$)
- Torque drops to **zero at synchronous speed** $N_s$
- Smooth, stable operation in the running region

**(b) A 220 V, 50 Hz split-phase induction motor has the following winding impedances at standstill: Main winding $Z_m = (4 + j7)\,\Omega$, Auxiliary winding $Z_a = (8 + j3)\,\Omega$. Determine: (i) the current in each winding, (ii) the phase angle between the two currents. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Currents in Each Winding:**

Main winding current magnitude:
$$I_m = \frac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{4^2 + 7^2}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{16 + 49}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{65}} = \frac{220}{8.062} = 27.29\,\text{A}$$

Phase angle of $I_m$ (lagging):
$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) = 60.26°$$

Auxiliary winding current magnitude:
$$I_a = \frac{V}{\vert Z_a \vert} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{8^2 + 3^2}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{64 + 9}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{73}} = \frac{220}{8.544} = 25.75\,\text{A}$$

Phase angle of $I_a$ (lagging):
$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{8}\right) = 20.56°$$

**(ii) Phase Angle Between Currents:**

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 60.26° - 20.56° = 39.70°$$

The auxiliary winding current **leads** the main winding current by **39.70°** — this is the phase split angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The phase split angle of 39.7° is on the higher end of the typical split-phase range (25°–35°). The high auxiliary winding resistance (8 Ω) is what creates this reasonable phase split without a capacitor.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2**

**(a) With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a capacitor-start single-phase induction motor. Why is the capacitor disconnected after starting? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram Description:**

The motor has two windings connected in parallel across the single-phase AC supply:
- **Main winding (M):** Low resistance, high reactance — remains in the circuit permanently.
- **Auxiliary winding (A):** In series with a **start capacitor** $C_{start}$ and a **centrifugal switch (CS)**, all connected in series with each other and parallel to the main winding.

**Working:**
- When the motor is energized, both the main and auxiliary windings carry current.
- The capacitor in the auxiliary branch causes the auxiliary current $I_a$ to **lead** the supply voltage significantly, while the main current $I_m$ **lags** the supply voltage.
- A phase split of **70° to 90°** is achieved between $I_a$ and $I_m$ — far superior to the split-phase motor.
- This large phase split, combined with the 90° spatial displacement, produces a strong **rotating magnetic field** and a high starting torque of **3× to 4.5× the full-load torque**.

**Why is the Capacitor Disconnected?**
1. The electrolytic start capacitor is **not designed for continuous operation** — it would overheat and fail if left in the circuit.
2. Once the rotor reaches 70%–80% of synchronous speed, the motor can run efficiently on the main winding alone.
3. The **centrifugal switch** opens automatically at this speed, disconnecting both the capacitor and the auxiliary winding from the supply.
4. This prolongs capacitor life and improves running efficiency.

**Applications:** Compressors (refrigeration, AC), water pumps, conveyors, machine tools, large fans requiring high starting torque.

**(b) A 230 V, 50 Hz capacitor-start single-phase induction motor has main winding impedance $Z_m = (3 + j5)\,\Omega$ and auxiliary winding impedance $Z_a = (6 + j2)\,\Omega$. A capacitor is connected in series with the auxiliary winding. Calculate the value of capacitance required for maximum starting torque. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the main winding current phase angle:
$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = 59.04°$$

**Step 2:** For **maximum starting torque**, the phase split angle $\alpha$ must equal **90°**. This means the auxiliary current must **lead** the main current by 90°, i.e., the auxiliary current must lead the supply voltage by:

$$\phi_a = -(90° - 59.04°) = -30.96°$$

The negative sign indicates the current **leads** the voltage (due to the capacitor).

**Step 3:** The auxiliary branch impedance with capacitor is:
$$Z_a^{total} = R_a + j(X_a - X_c) = 6 + j(2 - X_c)$$

For the current to lead by 30.96°:
$$\tan(\phi_a) = \frac{2 - X_c}{6} = \tan(-30.96°) = -0.6$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $X_c$:
$$2 - X_c = 6 \times (-0.6) = -3.6$$
$$X_c = 2 + 3.6 = 5.6\,\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the required capacitance:
$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f X_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 5.6} = \frac{1}{1759.29}$$

$$\boxed{C = 5.685 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{F} = 568.5\,\mu\text{F}}$$

**Verification:** A capacitance of 568.5 µF is large — typical of **electrolytic AC start capacitors** used in capacitor-start motors (range: 50–1000 µF depending on motor size).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
>
> 1. **Confusing forward and backward slip:** Many students write $s_b = s$ instead of $s_b = 2 - s$. Remember: the backward field rotates opposite to the rotor, so its slip is $(2 - s)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the centrifugal switch:** In capacitor-start motors, students often forget to mention the **centrifugal switch** and its role. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in KTU exams.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign in capacitance formula:** The formula $C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f [X_a - R_a\cot\phi_m]}$ is the most-mistyped formula. Re-derive it from $\tan\phi_a = (X_a - X_c)/R_a$ to avoid sign errors.
>
> 4. **Permanent Capacitor vs Capacitor-Start confusion:** Students mix up the two. Remember: **Capacitor-Start** has a LARGE electrolytic capacitor that's **disconnected** at 75% speed (high starting torque). **Permanent Capacitor** has a SMALL oil-filled capacitor that's **always in the circuit** (smooth running, high power factor).
>
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always state $C$ in **µF** (microfarads) or **mF** in the final answer, not in farads. Marks are deducted for missing unit specifications.
>
> 6. **Not labeling sketches:** In circuit diagrams, ALWAYS label the **main winding**, **auxiliary winding**, **capacitor**, and **centrifugal switch** clearly. Unlabeled diagrams fetch **partial credit only**.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Read this 30 minutes before the exam!**

✅ **Single-phase induction motors are NOT self-starting** because a pulsating flux can be resolved into two equal, opposite-rotating fields that cancel torque at standstill.

✅ **Double Revolving Field Theory (Boucherot):** $\phi_m\cos(\omega t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2}e^{j\omega t} + \frac{\phi_m}{2}e^{-j\omega t}$ — the foundation concept.

✅ **Two starting methods** work by creating a **phase split** between main and auxiliary winding currents:
- **Resistive split** (Split-Phase): 25°–35° phase split, low starting torque
- **Capacitive split** (Capacitor-Start, Permanent Capacitor): 70°–90° phase split, high performance

✅ **Slip Relations:** $s_f = s$, $s_b = 2 - s$ — memorize these!

✅ **Impedances:** $Z_f = R_2'/s + jX_2'$ and $Z_b = R_2'/(2-s) + jX_2'$

✅ **Starting Torque Formula:** $T_{st} \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$ — maximize when $\alpha = 90°$.

✅ **Capacitance Formula for Maximum $T_{st}$:** $C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f[X_a - R_a\cot\phi_m]}$

✅ **Centrifugal Switch:** Disconnects auxiliary winding (and start capacitor) at 70%–80% of $N_s$.

✅ **Applications Quick Recall:**
- Split-Phase → Washing machines, fans
- Capacitor-Start → Compressors, pumps
- Permanent Capacitor → Ceiling fans (very common in Indian households)

✅ **Power Factor:** Permanent Capacitor motors have the **best power factor** (near unity) because the run capacitor compensates for the inductive reactive power.

✅ **Always state whether a motor is starting-type or running-type** when answering KTU questions — examiners award marks for this distinction.

✅ **Cross-Field Theory** is an alternative to Double Revolving Field Theory; both yield identical results but the former is more intuitive for understanding torque production.
